proposals.title: Defining the Optimal Treatment Target in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Post-Hoc Analysis of RCTs Comparing Remission, Functional Improvement, and Radiographic Progression
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), remission (e.g., ASDAS-ID) is the established primary treatment target, but its universal applicability is debated. Many patients, especially those with long-standing disease or inadequate response to biologics, may not achieve remission despite effective therapy. Pre-2026, there is a growing need to determine if alternative targets, such as functional improvement (BASFI) or prevention of radiographic damage (mSASSS), are more meaningful and achievable primary goals for specific patient subgroups, potentially leading to more personalized treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific subgroups of axSpA patients (e.g., those with advanced radiographic damage, prior inadequate response to bDMARDs, or high baseline functional impairment), a treatment target based on a clinically meaningful improvement in function or the halting of radiographic progression is superior to remission in predicting long-term patient-relevant outcomes, including quality of life and work productivity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a post-hoc analysis of pooled, patient-level data from phase 3 RCTs of TNF inhibitors (e.g., adalimumab, secukinumab) and JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib) in active axSpA. Using multivariable logistic regression and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, we will model the association between achieving different 12-month targets (ASDAS-ID, ≥40% improvement in BASFI, no mSASSS progression) and 2-year outcomes (ASAS Health Index, SF-36 PCS, work instability). Subgroup analyses will be performed based on baseline characteristics, including symptom duration, HLA-B27 status, and prior bDMARD exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence-based guidance on whether the 'treat-to-target' paradigm in axSpA should be refined to incorporate alternative primary goals. It will inform future clinical trial design and treatment guidelines by identifying which patient profiles benefit most from a remission-centric versus a function- or damage-prevention-centric target, moving towards precision medicine in axSpA management.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has validated remission targets, this analysis directly challenges its primacy by systematically comparing it against other candidate targets using hard clinical trial data, focusing on patient subgroups often excluded from remission-focused strategies.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Personalized Monitoring Intervals Based on Disease Activity Trajectories in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Clinical Trial Data
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), frequent monitoring is standard but resource-intensive. With the proliferation of advanced therapies (e.g., TNFi, JAKi like upadacitinib), patients achieving stable remission may be over-monitored, while those with unstable disease may benefit from closer follow-up. Pre-2026, there is no evidence-based framework to tailor visit frequency, leading to potential inefficiency and patient burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stratifying patients by their early treatment response trajectory (e.g., rapid responder, slow responder, non-responder) can identify subgroups for whom extended monitoring intervals are non-inferior for detecting flares and more cost-effective than standard fixed-interval follow-up.
proposals.method: We will perform a post-hoc analysis of pooled, patient-level data from phase 3/4 trials of biologics/JAK inhibitors in axSpA (e.g., from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer, Lilly, Janssen). Using Group-Based Trajectory Modeling (GBTM), we will identify distinct disease activity (ASDAS/CRP) trajectories over the first 6 months. A discrete-event simulation model will then evaluate the cost-effectiveness of trajectory-stratified monitoring strategies (e.g., 6-month vs. 3-month intervals for stable responders) versus standard care, using flare rates and quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) from trial extensions and real-world data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, economic rationale for personalizing axSpA follow-up schedules. It will offer clinicians a practical algorithm to optimize clinic resource allocation and reduce patient burden without compromising outcomes, directly informing future treat-to-target and tapering strategies.
proposals.novelty: While trajectory modeling is used prognostically, its application to optimize healthcare delivery logistics (monitoring intervals) in rheumatology is novel. This moves beyond predicting drug response to actively designing a more efficient care pathway around the predicted response.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Open-Label, Treat-to-Target Trial Comparing Immediate Step-Up Therapy Versus Symptomatic Management in Patients with Early Oligoarthritis or Undifferentiated Arthritis
proposals.motivation: The treat-to-target (T2T) strategy is a cornerstone of management in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), improving long-term outcomes. However, its application in earlier, milder presentations like oligoarthritis or undifferentiated arthritis (UA) remains undefined. Given the variable prognosis of these conditions—some remit spontaneously while others progress to persistent, erosive disease—a structured, evidence-based approach is needed to prevent overtreatment and undertreatment.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formal treat-to-target strategy, with a target of clinical remission (Boolean or DAS28-CRP <2.6) and escalation to conventional synthetic or biologic DMARDs upon target failure, will lead to superior long-term functional and radiographic outcomes compared to a conventional, symptom-driven approach in patients with early oligoarthritis or UA.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, open-label, controlled trial. Patients with inflammatory oligoarthritis (≤4 swollen joints) or UA of <12 months duration will be randomized 1:1 to a T2T arm or a symptom-control arm. The T2T protocol mandates 3-monthly assessments; failure to reach the target (clinical remission) triggers a predefined step-up sequence: from NSAIDs → methotrexate → addition of sulfasalazine → escalation to a TNF inhibitor (e.g., adalimumab [AbbVie] or certolizumab [UCB]). The control arm receives NSAIDs and intra-articular corticosteroids at the physician's discretion. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients with no radiographic progression (mTSS change ≤0.5) and functional remission (HAQ-DI <0.5) at 24 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a structured management algorithm in early oligoarthritis/UA, a common clinical dilemma. It will define whether early, target-driven intervention alters the disease course, potentially preventing progression to persistent RA or other forms of chronic arthritis. The results will directly inform international treatment recommendations and clinical practice.
proposals.novelty: While T2T is established in RA, its systematic testing in the pre-RA or limited-joint stage is novel. This trial adapts the proven T2T methodology to a diagnostically uncertain, earlier disease phase, moving beyond the established paradigms in RA and axSpA for which Prof. van der Heijde has extensively researched treatment strategies and tapering.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Domain-Specific Treat-to-Target for Fatigue in Patients with Axial Spondyloarthritis: The DOMAIN-AxSpA Study
proposals.motivation: Current treat-to-target (T2T) strategies in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) focus on composite measures of disease activity (e.g., ASDAS, BASDAI), which may not adequately address debilitating patient-reported outcomes like fatigue. Fatigue is a major driver of reduced quality of life and work disability, yet its management remains unsystematic. A domain-specific T2T approach, targeting fatigue independently, could provide a more personalized and effective therapeutic strategy, aligning with patient priorities.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with axSpA and significant fatigue (BASDAI fatigue item ≥4) despite controlled inflammation (ASDAS < 2.1), a structured, domain-specific T2T intervention targeting fatigue will lead to superior improvements in fatigue levels compared to standard T2T based on global disease activity alone.
proposals.method: We propose a 12-month, multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial. Patients with active axSpA and predominant fatigue will be randomized 1:1 to Domain-T2T or Standard-T2T. In the Domain-T2T arm, treatment adjustments (escalation with biologics/JAK inhibitors like secukinumab, ixekizumab, or upadacitinib, based on prior exposure) will be triggered solely by a failure to achieve a pre-defined fatigue target (≥30% improvement in BASDAI fatigue item) at 3-month intervals, irrespective of ASDAS. The control arm follows conventional ASDAS-driven T2T. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving a ≥50% improvement in BASDAI fatigue at 12 months. Secondary endpoints include function (BASFI), pain, and work productivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the feasibility and efficacy of a symptom-domain-specific T2T strategy in rheumatology. It will generate a practical algorithm for managing fatigue in axSpA, potentially improving patient-centered care. The results could redefine T2T paradigms, shifting focus from composite indices to addressing the most burdensome individual symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While T2T is established, its application to a single, non-inflammatory domain like fatigue, decoupled from traditional inflammatory activity measures, is a novel and patient-centric concept. This moves beyond the current paradigm of adjusting therapy primarily for objective inflammation.

proposals.title: Real-world Radiographic Progression in Axial Spondyloarthritis Patients Treated with Tocilizumab Combination Therapies: A Multi-Centre Observational Study
proposals.motivation: While TNF inhibitors and JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib are established for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), real-world data on radiographic progression with IL-6 inhibition via tocilizumab (TCZ) is limited. Given the chronic, progressive nature of axSpA and the clinical need for effective treatment strategies after biologic DMARD failure, understanding the long-term structural impact of TCZ-based regimens in routine care is crucial for optimizing patient management.
proposals.hypothesis: In a real-world setting, patients with active axSpA and an inadequate response to prior biologic therapy who are treated with TCZ, either as monotherapy or in combination with conventional synthetic DMARDs, will demonstrate a significantly lower rate of radiographic spinal progression over two years compared to a matched historical cohort receiving conventional therapy.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-centre, retrospective, observational cohort study using data from established registries (e.g., GLADAR, BSRBR-RA adapted for axSpA) and clinical sites. Patients with axSpA (fulfilling ASAS criteria) treated with TCZ for ≥2 years will be identified. Radiographic progression will be assessed centrally by two blinded readers using the modified Stoke Ankylosing Spondylitis Spinal Score (mSASSS) on paired spinal radiographs (baseline and 2-year). Primary analysis will use a linear mixed model to estimate the mean change in mSASSS/year, adjusting for baseline damage, CRP, and concomitant csDMARD use. Propensity score matching will create a comparator cohort from pre-biologic era data within the same registries.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the potential of TCZ-based therapies to inhibit radiographic progression in axSpA, complementing existing RCT data on TNFi and JAKi. It will inform treatment sequencing and combination strategies in clinical practice, particularly for patients failing first-line biologics.
proposals.novelty: This focuses specifically on the structural outcomes of IL-6 pathway inhibition in axSpA, a less-studied mechanism, and utilizes robust, registry-based real-world methodology to assess long-term progression, an area where data for TCZ is sparse compared to agents from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer, and Lilly.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Open-Label, Head-to-Head Trial Comparing Tocilizumab plus Leflunomide versus TNF Inhibitors in Biologic-Naïve Rheumatoid Arthritis Patients with Methotrexate Intolerance or Contraindication
proposals.motivation: Methotrexate (MTX) intolerance or contraindication is a common clinical challenge in rheumatoid arthritis (RA), forcing a shift to biologic therapies, often without a conventional synthetic DMARD backbone. Pre-2026, head-to-head trials comparing treatment strategies in this specific, clinically relevant subpopulation are lacking, leaving rheumatologists without evidence-based guidance on optimal biologic selection when MTX cannot be used.
proposals.hypothesis: In MTX-intolerant/contraindicated, biologic-naïve RA patients, the combination of tocilizumab (TCZ) with leflunomide (LEF) will be non-inferior to TNF inhibitors (adalimumab or etanercept) in achieving low disease activity at 24 weeks, and superior in reducing pain as measured by patient-reported outcomes.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, randomized, open-label, assessor-blinded, parallel-group trial. Biologic-naïve RA patients with active disease and documented MTX intolerance/contraindication will be randomized 1:1 to receive either subcutaneous TCZ plus LEF or a TNF inhibitor (TNFi) monotherapy (adalimumab or etanercept). The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving DAS28-ESR low disease activity (≤3.2) at 24 weeks, tested for non-inferiority (margin of 12%). Key secondary endpoints include change in pain VAS, HAQ-DI, and ASAS40 response in patients with axial involvement. Statistical analysis will use a modified ITT population and logistic regression adjusted for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct comparative effectiveness data for two major biologic strategies in the underserved MTX-intolerant population, informing EULAR and ACR management guidelines. It will specifically address whether a targeted IL-6 pathway inhibition combined with an alternative csDMARD offers advantages over TNF inhibition in controlling disease activity and the prominent symptom of pain in these patients.
proposals.novelty: While TCZ+MTX and TNFi+MTX have been compared, a direct comparison in an MTX-free context, using LEF as a pragmatic alternative backbone with TCZ, is novel. The exclusive focus on MTX-intolerant patients addresses a critical evidence gap not tackled by prior major RCTs which required or allowed MTX co-therapy.

proposals.title: A Cost-Utility Analysis of Tocilizumab plus Methotrexate versus Tocilizumab plus Leflunomide in Patients with Rheumatoid Arthritis: An Integrated Economic Evaluation from a Randomized Clinical Trial
proposals.motivation: In rheumatoid arthritis (RA), the high cost of biologic and targeted synthetic DMARDs necessitates rigorous cost-effectiveness analyses to inform treatment guidelines and reimbursement decisions. While the efficacy of tocilizumab (TCZ) with methotrexate (MTX) is established, the economic value of TCZ combined with alternative conventional synthetic DMARDs like leflunomide (LEF) remains unexplored, leaving a critical evidence gap for patients intolerant to MTX.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, within a cohort achieving comparable clinical efficacy, the combination of TCZ+LEF will demonstrate non-inferior cost-effectiveness compared to TCZ+MTX, driven by differences in drug acquisition costs, monitoring requirements, and management of adverse events.
proposals.method: This study will perform a prospective cost-utility analysis alongside a randomized, open-label, non-inferiority clinical trial comparing TCZ+MTX versus TCZ+LEF in RA patients over a 2-year time horizon. Healthcare resource utilization (medication, monitoring, hospitalizations) will be captured prospectively. Quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) will be derived from EQ-5D-5L questionnaires administered at regular intervals. A Markov microsimulation model will be developed to extrapolate long-term costs and outcomes, using Dutch healthcare payer perspective and discount rates as per ISPOR guidelines. Deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses will assess parameter uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, trial-based economic comparison of two key TCZ combination strategies, offering evidence to optimize treatment sequencing and healthcare resource allocation in RA. It will deliver a validated economic model that can be adapted for local cost inputs to support national health technology assessment (HTA) submissions.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness of TCZ+MTX versus other biologics has been studied, a head-to-head economic evaluation of TCZ with different anchor DMARDs within the same clinical cohort is novel and addresses a pragmatic clinical question of therapeutic substitution due to intolerance.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Immediate Conventional Synthetic DMARDs versus Initial NSAID Therapy on Long-term Radiographic and Functional Outcomes in Early Psoriatic Arthritis (The DIRECT-PsA Trial)
proposals.motivation: Current EULAR and GRAPPA treatment guidelines for psoriatic arthritis (PsA) recommend NSAIDs as first-line therapy for peripheral arthritis, reserving csDMARDs for patients with poor prognostic factors or inadequate response. This step-up approach, while pragmatic, may delay effective disease-modifying treatment in a significant proportion of patients, potentially allowing irreversible joint damage and functional decline to accrue. Given the proven efficacy of early, aggressive intervention in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) and rheumatoid arthritis (RA), a paradigm shift to rapid csDMARD initiation in all early PsA patients warrants investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with newly diagnosed, active PsA, immediate initiation of a csDMARD (methotrexate) without a prior NSAID trial will lead to superior long-term radiographic and functional outcomes at 2 years, compared to a standard step-up approach starting with NSAIDs.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-center, randomized, open-label, parallel-group trial. Patients with early, active PsA (diagnosis <24 months, ≥3 tender/swollen joints) and no prior csDMARD exposure will be randomized 1:1 to: 1) Immediate csDMARD arm: methotrexate (or leflunomide/sulfasalazine if contraindicated) initiated at baseline, or 2) Step-up arm: a 4-week trial of maximally tolerated NSAID therapy, with csDMARD initiation only if minimal disease activity (MDA) is not achieved. The primary endpoint will be the change in the PsA-modified Sharp/van der Heijde score from baseline to 104 weeks. Key secondary endpoints include the proportion of patients achieving MDA, HAQ-DI scores, and time to first bDMARD initiation. Analysis will be by intention-to-treat, using linear mixed models for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This trial will provide the first high-level evidence to either validate or challenge the universal use of NSAIDs as initial therapy in early PsA. It will directly inform updates to international treatment guidelines, potentially shifting the standard of care towards a more proactive, treat-to-target strategy from diagnosis. The results will clarify the risk-benefit balance of rapid csDMARD use regarding long-term structural damage, function, and the need for costly biologic therapies.
proposals.novelty: While treatment de-escalation studies (like Dr. van der Heijde's work in RA) and rapid biologic initiation in axSpA exist, no trial has prospectively challenged the foundational 'NSAID-first' step in early PsA management. This study directly tests the necessity of this therapeutic delay, a concept not yet evaluated in the PsA literature as of 2025.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Open-Label, Head-to-Head Comparison of TNF, IL-17A, and IL-12/23 Inhibitors on Distinct Psoriatic Arthritis Disease Domains: The COMPARE-PsA Trial
proposals.motivation: Despite the availability of multiple biologic DMARD (bDMARD) classes for psoriatic arthritis (PsA), including TNF inhibitors (e.g., adalimumab), IL-17A inhibitors (e.g., secukinumab), and IL-12/23 inhibitors (e.g., ustekinumab), direct comparative efficacy data are scarce. Treatment decisions are based on class-level efficacy assumptions rather than robust head-to-head evidence, particularly regarding differential effects on key PsA domains like peripheral arthritis, axial disease, enthesitis, dactylitis, and skin psoriasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TNF, IL-17A, and IL-12/23 inhibitors will demonstrate significantly different efficacy profiles across the distinct clinical domains of PsA, with no single agent class being superior across all domains.
proposals.method: We propose a 52-week, multicenter, randomized, open-label, assessor-blinded, three-arm trial. Adults with active PsA (CASPAR criteria) and an inadequate response to csDMARDs will be randomized 1:1:1 to receive standard doses of a representative from each class: adalimumab (TNFi), secukinumab (IL-17Ai), or ustekinumab (IL-12/23i). The primary endpoint will be the difference in change from baseline in a composite domain-specific score at week 24, incorporating weighted outcomes for peripheral joint count (SJC66/TJC68), spinal pain (BASDAI question 2), enthesitis (SPARCC), dactylitis (LDI), and skin (PASI). Secondary endpoints include ACR20/50/70, MDA, and ASAS40 responses at weeks 24 and 52. Statistical analysis will employ a linear mixed model for repeated measures (MMRM) to compare the domain-specific composite score and individual components between treatment arms, with adjustment for stratification factors (presence of axial symptoms, prior TNFi exposure).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, head-to-head evidence of the comparative effectiveness of the three major bDMARD classes across the full spectrum of PsA manifestations. The results will enable a more precise, domain-driven treatment strategy, moving beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' approach. It will inform international treatment guidelines (e.g., GRAPPA) and support personalized medicine by matching drug mechanism to predominant disease phenotype.
proposals.novelty: While network meta-analyses have attempted indirect comparisons, no previous randomized trial has directly compared these three distinct bDMARD mechanisms simultaneously in PsA with a pre-specified, domain-specific primary outcome. The novel composite endpoint is designed to capture the multidimensional treatment effect in a single, clinically meaningful measure.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial Comparing a TNF Inhibitor Tapering Strategy versus Standard-of-Care in Axial Spondyloarthritis Patients with Predominant Axial Disease and Sustained Remission: The OPTIMUM-AxSpA Study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), achieving remission is a key goal, but optimal long-term management after remission is unclear. Current guidelines lack specific recommendations for treatment de-escalation in patients with predominant axial disease, leading to potential overtreatment and unnecessary cost and risk. This gap is critical to address as biologics and JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib show high efficacy, raising the question of whether stable, indefinite therapy is always necessary.
proposals.hypothesis: In axSpA patients with predominant axial disease who have achieved sustained remission (ASDAS <1.3 for ≥6 months) on a stable TNF inhibitor dose, a protocolized, gradual tapering strategy will be non-inferior to maintaining a stable dose in preventing disease flares (ASDAS ≥2.1) over 12 months.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Patients will be randomized 1:1 to a protocolized taper (e.g., extending dosing intervals by 50% every 12 weeks) or stable standard-of-care. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients experiencing a flare (ASDAS ≥2.1) within 12 months, with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 15%. Secondary endpoints include time to flare, pain (BASDAI pain item), function (BASFI), and imaging progression (MRI SPARCC score for spine/sacroiliac joints). Statistical analysis will use a modified intention-to-treat approach with a logistic regression model adjusted for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a safe and effective treatment tapering strategy specifically in axSpA with predominant axial involvement. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on treatment optimization, potentially reducing patient burden and healthcare costs. The results will also offer insights into the natural history of treated, remissive disease and the feasibility of dose reduction as a treatment goal.
proposals.novelty: While van der Heijde's prior work tested tapering in rheumatoid arthritis, this trial specifically targets the distinct pathophysiology and clinical course of predominant axial disease in axSpA. It moves beyond efficacy of new drugs (e.g., upadacitinib) to address the critical, unanswered 'what next?' question in disease management for patients doing well.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Open-Label, Non-Inferiority Trial of Upadacitinib Monotherapy Versus Upadacitinib Plus Methotrexate in Biologic-Naïve Patients with Active Axial Spondyloarthritis
proposals.motivation: Methotrexate (MTX) is a cornerstone first-line csDMARD in rheumatoid arthritis, but its efficacy in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is limited, primarily for peripheral symptoms. Despite this, MTX is often empirically prescribed as an anchor drug before or alongside advanced therapies like JAK inhibitors, potentially exposing patients to unnecessary toxicity without proven axial benefit. With the proven efficacy of JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib in axSpA, it is critical to challenge the universal necessity of MTX co-therapy in this specific disease context.
proposals.hypothesis: In biologic-naïve patients with active axSpA, upadacitinib monotherapy will be non-inferior to upadacitinib combined with methotrexate for achieving ASAS40 response at one year, while demonstrating a superior safety and tolerability profile.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Biologic-naïve patients with active axSpA (meeting ASAS criteria) will be randomized 1:1 to receive upadacitinib 15mg daily alone or in combination with stable-dose oral MTX (≥15mg/week). The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving ASAS40 response at Week 52. A pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 12% will be used. Secondary endpoints include ASDAS-CRP, SPARCC MRI scores for sacroiliac joints and spine, patient-reported pain (assessed via VAS), and safety/tolerability measures (including liver function tests, nausea, and drop-out rates). Statistical analysis will follow the intention-to-treat principle.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on whether MTX co-therapy is necessary with a highly effective advanced therapy in axSpA. It will directly inform treatment guidelines, potentially simplifying regimens, improving patient adherence, and reducing MTX-related adverse events. The results could shift the paradigm away from a reflexive 'anchor drug' strategy towards more targeted, mechanism-based monotherapy in axial disease.
proposals.novelty: While MTX tapering has been studied in RA remission (as in Dr. van der Heijde's prior work), this trial proactively challenges its initial use in a disease (axSpA) where its foundational efficacy is questionable, using a modern JAK inhibitor comparator. It moves beyond observational data to a direct, randomized test of a deeply entrenched clinical habit.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Safety of a Treat-to-Target Strategy in Elderly Axial Spondyloarthritis Patients with Comorbidities: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Treat-to-target (T2T) is a cornerstone of modern rheumatology, but its applicability in specific subgroups like elderly patients and those with comorbidities remains unvalidated. Pre-2026 evidence from trials of drugs like TNF inhibitors (Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer) and JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, Lilly) primarily involves younger, healthier populations. Given the aging population and the high prevalence of comorbidities like cardiovascular disease in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), there is an urgent need to test if T2T is safe and effective in real-world, complex patients.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured treat-to-target strategy, using ASAS-defined treatment targets and a protocolized escalation algorithm including advanced therapies (b/tsDMARDs), is non-inferior for disease control but associated with a different safety profile in elderly axSpA patients with comorbidities compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, open-label, randomized controlled non-inferiority trial. Patients aged ≥65 years with active axSpA and ≥1 major comorbidity will be randomized to a protocol-driven T2T arm (target: ASDAS <2.1) or a routine care arm. The intervention uses a pre-defined escalation algorithm, informed by recent trials (e.g., upadacitinib in bDMARD-inadequate responders), guiding therapy from NSAIDs to conventional and then biologic/targeted synthetic DMARDs (consulting ASAS/EULAR recommendations). Primary outcome is the proportion of patients achieving ASDAS low disease activity at 52 weeks. Key safety outcomes include serious infections and major adverse cardiovascular events, analyzed using mixed-effects models for repeated measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the feasibility of T2T in a high-risk, understudied axSpA population. It will generate crucial data on the benefit-risk balance of intensive treatment strategies in the elderly, directly informing clinical guidelines and shared decision-making. The results will clarify whether current treatment paradigms, driven by trials in younger adults, require modification for older, comorbid patients.
proposals.novelty: While T2T is established, its rigorous testing in the complex elderly axSpA population with protocolized use of the latest drug classes (JAK inhibitors) represents a significant gap. This moves beyond general efficacy studies (like those for upadacitinib) to test a holistic management strategy in the patients most challenging to treat.

proposals.title: A Phase III, Randomised, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Evaluate the Efficacy and Safety of Upadacitinib in Preventing the Onset of Clinical Rheumatoid Arthritis in At-Risk Individuals with Arthralgia and Anti-Citrullinated Protein Antibodies
proposals.motivation: Preventing the onset of clinical rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in at-risk individuals is a critical, unmet need. The SPACE cohort work demonstrates the importance of early diagnosis in spondyloarthritis, and similar principles apply to RA, where autoantibody-positive arthralgia represents a pre-clinical, high-risk state. Current strategies focus on treatment after diagnosis; intervening earlier could potentially avert irreversible joint damage and lifelong disability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 12-month course of upadacitinib, a Janus kinase inhibitor, will be superior to placebo in preventing or significantly delaying the development of clinically apparent RA (according to 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria) in individuals with arthralgia and high-risk autoantibody profiles.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-centre, randomised controlled trial. At-risk individuals (arthralgia, positive for anti-CCP antibodies, and with subclinical inflammation on MRI of hands/feet) will be randomised 1:1 to upadacitinib 15mg once daily or placebo for 12 months, followed by a 12-month observation period. The primary endpoint is the development of clinical synovitis fulfilling 2010 RA criteria within 24 months. Secondary endpoints include changes in MRI inflammation scores, patient-reported outcomes (pain, function), and safety. Statistical analysis will use a time-to-event (Cox proportional hazards) model for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the efficacy and safety of a targeted DMARD for primary prevention of RA. It will establish whether ultra-early intervention in the 'pre-RA' phase can alter the disease course, potentially defining a new therapeutic paradigm. The results will inform clinical guidelines on the management of at-risk individuals.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have tested conventional DMARDs like methotrexate in at-risk populations, this is the first to investigate a JAK inhibitor in this context, leveraging its rapid onset of action. Furthermore, it directly applies the 'treat-to-target' and early intervention logic from established diseases like axSpA (as in the researcher's work) to the pre-clinical phase of RA.

proposals.title: Predicting Sustained Remission in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Machine Learning Analysis of Baseline Clinical and Treatment Factors in the SPACE and Other Prospective Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Achieving sustained remission is a primary treatment goal in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), but it remains unpredictable which patients will attain this state. While clinical trials for drugs like TNF inhibitors (e.g., from AbbVie, Pfizer, Novartis) and JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib from AbbVie) report remission rates, real-world predictive models are lacking. Identifying reliable baseline predictors is crucial for personalized treatment strategies and early intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of baseline clinical features (e.g., symptom duration, CRP, ASDAS), patient demographics, and initial treatment choice (bDMARD vs. conventional therapy) will accurately predict the probability of achieving sustained ASDAS inactive disease or ASAS partial remission over 2 years.
proposals.method: We will apply supervised machine learning algorithms, primarily random forest and LASSO regression, to high-quality, longitudinal data from the SPACE cohort and other axSpA registries. The outcome is sustained remission (≥6 months). The models will be trained on a comprehensive set of baseline variables, including clinical metrics, HLA-B27 status, imaging, and first-line treatment. Model performance will be evaluated using area-under-the-curve (AUC) and calibration plots, with internal validation via bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically actionable prediction model for sustained remission in axSpA, aiding rheumatologists in prognostication and shared decision-making. It will also identify modifiable baseline factors, offering insights for refining treat-to-target strategies and informing future trial design for agents like IL-17 inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous work focusing on response to specific drugs, this study addresses the inverse problem of predicting a long-term holistic outcome (sustained remission) from baseline status, integrating a broad range of real-world clinical and treatment initiation data using contemporary machine learning techniques.

proposals.title: Prospective validation of baseline MRI and ultrasound for predicting individual response to TNF inhibitors and JAK inhibitors in axial spondyloarthritis: the IMAGINE-AxSpA study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), treatment decisions, including the use of TNF inhibitors (TNFi) or newer agents like JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib), are often based on clinical scores, with significant variability in individual patient response. Pre-2026, imaging biomarkers like MRI bone marrow edema (BME) and ultrasound enthesitis are recognized as key pathophysiological features, but their prospective utility for predicting treatment outcomes in individual patients remains unvalidated. This leads to a trial-and-error approach, delaying effective therapy and incurring unnecessary costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite baseline imaging score, integrating spinal/sacroiliac joint MRI BME and peripheral enthesitis on ultrasound, will prospectively identify patients with active axSpA who are most likely to achieve a major clinical response (ASAS40) to TNFi or JAK inhibitor therapy at one year.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center, observational cohort study of 300 bDMARD-naïve axSpA patients initiating a TNFi (e.g., adalimumab, secukinumab) or JAK inhibitor (upadacitinib). All patients will undergo standardized baseline MRI of the spine/sacroiliac joints and a 12-site ultrasound enthesitis assessment (GUESS). Imaging will be centrally scored using the SPARCC method for MRI and the Madrid Sonography Enthesitis Index (MASEI) for ultrasound. A multivariable logistic regression model will be developed to predict ASAS40 response at 52 weeks, with the composite imaging score as the primary predictor, adjusted for clinical covariates (BASDAI, CRP, HLA-B27). Model performance will be assessed using area under the ROC curve (AUC), calibration plots, and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospectively validated imaging-based prediction model for treatment response in axSpA, moving beyond associative findings to actionable clinical tools. It will offer rheumatologists a data-driven method to stratify patients at treatment initiation, potentially improving first-line therapy success rates. The results could inform future treatment guidelines and the design of stratified clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked imaging findings to disease activity, this study prospectively validates a pre-treatment imaging composite as a predictive—not just diagnostic or monitoring—tool for specific therapeutic outcomes. It directly addresses the clinical translation gap by testing imaging in a real-world treatment decision context, integrating both axial (MRI) and peripheral (ultrasound) components relevant to axSpA.

proposals.title: Predicting Long-Term Radiographic Progression and Disability in Early Axial Spondyloarthritis: A 10-Year Follow-up of the SPACE Cohort Using Baseline MRI and Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), early detection and intervention are crucial to prevent irreversible structural damage and disability. However, pre-2026, the long-term prognostic value of early MRI findings (e.g., bone marrow edema, fatty lesions) and clinical factors for predicting radiographic progression (mSASSS) and functional outcome (BASFI) remains incompletely defined, hindering personalized treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific baseline MRI lesion patterns, combined with clinical and serological biomarkers (e.g., CRP, ASDAS), can predict the 10-year trajectory of radiographic spinal progression and functional disability in patients with early axSpA.
proposals.method: This is a long-term, observational cohort study extending the pre-existing SPACE cohort. Patients with early chronic back pain (<2 years) and a baseline sacroiliac/spine MRI will be followed for 10 years with regular clinical and radiographic (conventional X-rays for mSASSS) assessments. We will employ multivariable logistic and linear mixed-effects regression models to identify baseline predictors. Machine learning techniques, specifically random forest classifiers, will be used to develop a predictive model for 'rapid progressors' versus 'non-progressors', integrating MRI features (scored using the SPARCC or Berlin methods), clinical variables (e.g., smoking, HLA-B27), and treatment exposure (including TNFi, IL-17i, and JAK inhibitors).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term, evidence-based prognostic model for axSpA derived from a true early inception cohort. It will directly inform clinical practice by identifying patients at highest risk for poor outcomes who may benefit from early, intensive treatment (e.g., with biologics from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer, Lilly, or Janssen). The findings will also refine future clinical trial design by enabling better patient stratification.
proposals.novelty: While MRI is used for diagnosis, its specific prognostic value over a decade in a rigorously defined early axSpA cohort is unknown. This study uniquely integrates long-term radiographic and disability outcomes with comprehensive baseline imaging and clinical data to move beyond diagnostic utility toward true prediction of disease course.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Deep Learning Model for Automated SPARCC and Berlin MRI Scoring in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Multicenter Study
proposals.motivation: In clinical trials for axSpA, such as those evaluating TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, and JAK inhibitors, precise and consistent scoring of MRI inflammation (e.g., SPARCC, Berlin scores) is critical for assessing treatment efficacy. Manual scoring is time-consuming, subject to intra- and inter-reader variability, and a bottleneck in high-volume research settings. An automated, validated tool would standardize assessment, directly supporting the evaluation of therapies from sponsors like Novartis, AbbVie, and Pfizer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on a large, multicenter dataset of sacroiliac joint and spine MRIs can achieve non-inferior agreement with consensus expert radiologist readings for SPARCC and Berlin inflammation scores, compared to the agreement between human readers.
proposals.method: We will utilize a multi-stage deep learning approach. First, a U-Net architecture will segment relevant anatomical structures (sacroiliac joints, vertebral corners) on T1-weighted and STIR sequences from the DESIR and SPACE cohorts. Second, a ResNet-50 classifier will identify and score inflammatory lesions (bone marrow edema) within these regions. The model will be trained and validated on a dataset of ~2000 paired baseline and follow-up MRIs from existing axSpA trials, with ground truth defined by consensus of three central readers. Performance will be tested for agreement (ICC) and sensitivity to change.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully automated, validated software tool for quantifying axSpA inflammation on MRI, enabling faster, more reproducible outcome assessment in clinical trials and routine practice. It will directly facilitate research on treatment strategies, including tapering and switching of biologics, by providing an objective, efficient measure of disease activity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on binary detection of lesions, this proposal aims for full, granular score prediction (SPARCC, Berlin) that matches clinical trial endpoints. The integration of segmentation and classification in a single pipeline, trained on a large, heterogeneous trial dataset, moves beyond proof-of-concept towards a deployable solution for the rheumatology research community.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial of Methotrexate and Sulfasalazine versus Methotrexate, Sulfasalazine, and Prednisolone for Early Disease Control in Active Axial Spondyloarthritis: The STEP-DOWN Study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), early and effective control of inflammation is critical to prevent structural damage and long-term disability. While conventional synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs) like methotrexate and sulfasalazine are used, clinical practice often relies on bridging corticosteroids for rapid symptom relief, despite their well-known long-term adverse effects. A pre-2026 evidence gap exists regarding whether a csDMARD combination alone can achieve comparable early efficacy to a regimen including prednisolone, which would offer a safer initial strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with active axial spondyloarthritis, a combination of methotrexate and sulfasalazine (without initial prednisolone) is non-inferior to the same combination with a tapering course of prednisolone for achieving a clinically important improvement in disease activity (ASAS40) at 12 weeks.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled non-inferiority trial. Patients with active axSpA (meeting ASAS criteria, BASDAI ≥4, and objective inflammation via CRP or MRI) who are naïve to b/tsDMARDs will be randomized 1:1 to receive: 1) methotrexate (up to 25 mg/week) + sulfasalazine (up to 3 g/day) + a 12-week tapering regimen of oral prednisolone (starting at 20 mg/day), or 2) the same csDMARD regimen + matched placebo tablets. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving an ASAS40 response at week 12. Key secondary endpoints include change in BASDAI, ASDAS, and CRP at weeks 4, 12, and 24. Statistical analysis will follow the intention-to-treat principle, using a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 15%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to guide the initial pharmacological management of active axSpA, specifically addressing the necessity of corticosteroids. It will inform treatment guidelines on whether a csDMARD-first strategy can achieve rapid control, potentially reducing steroid-related morbidity. The results could redefine a safer standard initial approach, especially in healthcare settings with limited access to advanced therapies.
proposals.novelty: While corticosteroid-sparing strategies have been explored in rheumatoid arthritis, a dedicated, rigorous trial testing the omission of prednisolone in a csDMARD combination for early axSpA is lacking. This study directly challenges the clinical axiom that corticosteroids are required for initial control in this population.

proposals.title: Discordance Between One-Year Clinical Response and Five-Year Radiographic and Functional Outcomes in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trial Data
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), treatment decisions, including dose tapering or switching, are often guided by short-term (e.g., 12-16 week) clinical response metrics like ASDAS or BASDAI. However, the primary therapeutic goal is long-term disease modification, preventing structural progression and functional decline. It is unclear whether achieving a state of low disease activity or remission at one year reliably predicts sustained long-term benefit, particularly for radiographic outcomes, creating a critical evidence gap for treat-to-target strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of axSpA patients who achieve a state of low disease activity or clinical remission at one year on advanced therapies (TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) will nonetheless exhibit meaningful radiographic progression or functional deterioration over five years, indicating a disconnect between short-term symptom control and long-term disease modification.
proposals.method: We will perform a pooled, post-hoc analysis of patient-level data from phase 3 RCTs and their open-label extensions for drugs like secukinumab (Novartis), adalimumab (AbbVie), upadacitinib (AbbVie), and ixekizumab (Lilly) in axSpA. Patients will be stratified by their one-year clinical response status (e.g., ASDAS <2.1). The primary outcome is the proportion of 'one-year responders' with radiographic progression (mSASSS increase ≥2) at five years. Secondary analyses will use multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors (e.g., baseline CRP, MRI inflammation, smoking) of this discordance and assess functional outcomes (BASFI).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative evidence on the predictive value of a one-year treatment milestone for long-term structural outcomes in axSpA. It will inform more nuanced treat-to-target strategies, potentially identifying patient subgroups that require closer monitoring or different treatment targets beyond clinical symptoms. The findings will be crucial for guideline development and shared decision-making regarding long-term therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., van der Heijde's TNFi tapering trial in RA) has examined flare risk after treatment reduction, this proposal directly challenges the assumed correlation between intermediate-term clinical response and the ultimate goal of disease modification in axSpA, using a novel synthesis of long-term trial data across multiple drug classes to test this foundational clinical assumption.

proposals.title: Long-term Impact of Early Combination Therapy with TNF Inhibitors and Conventional DMARDs on Functional Disability and Radiographic Progression in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A 10-Year Follow-up of the SPACE Cohort
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), early diagnosis and treatment are crucial, but the optimal initial therapeutic strategy remains debated. While TNF inhibitors (TNFi) from companies like AbbVie, Pfizer, and Novartis are effective, the long-term benefit of starting with combination therapy (TNFi plus conventional synthetic DMARDs) versus TNFi monotherapy early in the disease course is unknown. Assessing long-term outcomes is essential to inform treatment guidelines and prevent irreversible structural damage and disability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with early axSpA who initiated combination therapy at diagnosis will exhibit significantly less radiographic progression and lower levels of long-term functional disability after 10 years compared to those who started with TNFi monotherapy or conventional DMARDs alone.
proposals.method: This is a longitudinal observational cohort study extending the SPONDYLOARTHRITIS CAUGHT EARLY (SPACE) cohort. We will perform a 10-year clinical and radiographic follow-up of patients stratified by their initial treatment strategy. Primary outcomes are change in modified Stoke Ankylosing Spondylitis Spinal Score (mSASSS) for radiographic progression and functional disability measured by the Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Functional Index (BASFI). We will use mixed-effects models for repeated measures (MMRM) to analyze trajectories, adjusting for baseline disease activity, CRP, and HLA-B27 status. Propensity score matching will address potential confounding by indication in treatment allocation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term, real-world evidence on the impact of initial treatment intensity in early axSpA. It will directly inform clinical practice on whether early combination therapy offers a durable advantage in preserving function and spinal structure. The results will be critical for health economic evaluations and treatment recommendations by bodies like ASAS.
proposals.novelty: While trials like RAPID-axSpA established short-term efficacy of TNFi, and the SPACE cohort addressed early diagnosis, no study has specifically tracked the long-term radiographic and functional outcomes of initial combination therapy versus monotherapy strategies from the point of early diagnosis in a real-world setting.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Weighted Composite Endpoint for Rheumatoid Arthritis Integrating Disability, Pain, and Radiographic Progression: A Post-Hoc Analysis of TNFi and JAKi Clinical Trials
proposals.motivation: Current composite endpoints in rheumatoid arthritis (RA), such as ACR20/50/70 and DAS28, often prioritize inflammatory activity and may not fully capture the multidimensional impact of the disease, particularly long-term structural damage and patient-reported outcomes like pain and disability. This limits their utility in trials assessing long-term disease modification and treatment strategies, such as dose tapering, which is a key clinical question as evidenced by recent trials in remission. A more holistic endpoint is needed to better evaluate the overall benefit-risk profile of therapies, including newer agents like JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib) and established TNF inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel, weighted composite endpoint incorporating the Health Assessment Questionnaire-Disability Index (HAQ-DI), patient-reported pain (VAS), and radiographic progression (e.g., van der Heijde-modified Sharp Score) can be developed and validated to provide a more comprehensive and sensitive measure of overall disease impact in RA clinical trials compared to existing endpoints.
proposals.method: We will apply methodology from cardiovascular outcome trials (e.g., the win ratio, or a pre-specified weighted composite) to individual patient data from existing phase 3/4 RA trials of TNFi (e.g., adalimumab, etanercept) and JAKi (e.g., upadacitinib, tofacitinib). Using data from trials like the one on TNFi tapering, we will first derive optimal weights for HAQ-DI, pain VAS, and radiographic score change through expert consensus (ASAS/OMERACT frameworks) and statistical techniques (e.g., principal component analysis). The endpoint's performance will be validated by assessing its discriminative ability, sensitivity to change, and correlation with functional outcomes in independent trial datasets, comparing it to standard endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, multidimensional tool for RA trials that better reflects the patient's total disease experience, facilitating more nuanced comparisons between therapeutic strategies, including dose optimization and sequencing of biologics/JAK inhibitors. It will offer a standardized outcome measure for future studies aiming to demonstrate comprehensive disease control beyond mere inflammation suppression.
proposals.novelty: While composite endpoints are standard in cardiology, their tailored application to integrate patient-centric (pain, disability) and objective (radiographic) domains in RA is novel. This approach moves beyond inflammation-focused composites to create an endpoint specifically designed for assessing long-term, holistic treatment success in the era of targeted therapies.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, International, Cluster-Randomized Trial to Validate a Treat-to-Target Strategy for Axial Spondyloarthritis Across Diverse Healthcare Settings and Patient Populations
proposals.motivation: Treat-to-target (T2T) is a cornerstone of modern rheumatology, but its validation has primarily occurred in controlled trials within well-resourced, homogeneous settings. Its efficacy, feasibility, and impact on outcomes like pain and function in diverse real-world populations—varying by healthcare system, socioeconomic status, and ethnicity—remain unproven. This gap limits the generalizability of current management guidelines for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA).
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a standardized, protocol-driven T2T strategy, targeting ASDAS low disease activity or remission, will lead to superior clinical outcomes compared to usual care in patients with axSpA across diverse international settings, and this benefit will be achievable regardless of regional healthcare system constraints or patient demographic factors.
proposals.method: We propose a 2-year, international, cluster-randomized, pragmatic trial. Rheumatology clinics across varied healthcare systems (e.g., Europe, North America, Asia, South America) will be randomized to implement a protocolized T2T strategy (intervention) or continue usual care (control). The T2T protocol will use a predefined, step-up algorithm based on ASAS-ASDAS targets, incorporating conventional and advanced therapies (e.g., TNF inhibitors like adalimumab [AbbVie], secukinumab [Novartis], and JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib [AbbVie]), with treatment decisions triggered at 3-month assessments. Primary outcome: proportion of patients achieving ASDAS <2.1 at 12 months. Secondary outcomes include feasibility metrics (protocol adherence, time to target), pain (ASAS pain scale), function (BASFI), and quality of life, analyzed with mixed-effects models to account for clustering and adjust for demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the real-world effectiveness and practical implementation of T2T in axSpA across global contexts. It will identify modifiable barriers (e.g., access to biologics, visit frequency) and facilitators to successful T2T, informing the development of adaptable, equitable treatment paradigms. The results will directly guide updates to international ASAS/EULAR management recommendations.
proposals.novelty: While T2T principles are established, this is the first study designed explicitly to test its validity across the full spectrum of healthcare delivery models and patient diversity, moving beyond proof-of-concept to prove robustness and generalizability in a pragmatic trial framework that mirrors real-world complexities.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Multimodal Risk Score for Early Escalation to bDMARD Therapy in Patients with Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Post-Hoc Analysis of the SPACE and DESIR Cohorts
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), treatment guidelines recommend starting with NSAIDs and csDMARDs like sulfasalazine, escalating to bDMARDs (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) only after failure. This 'step-up' approach may delay effective therapy for patients with aggressive disease, leading to irreversible structural damage and persistent pain. Pre-2026, there is a critical need for validated, clinically applicable tools to identify at diagnosis which patients are unlikely to respond to conventional therapy and would benefit from immediate bDMARD initiation, optimizing outcomes and cost-effectiveness.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite risk score integrating specific baseline clinical, imaging (MRI), and serological (CRP, HLA-B27) factors can accurately predict failure to achieve ASAS40 response or significant MRI inflammation reduction after 6 months of conventional therapy, thereby identifying candidates for first-line bDMARD use.
proposals.method: We will perform a post-hoc analysis of two well-characterized early axSpA cohorts (SPACE and DESIR). Using baseline data, we will employ machine learning (LASSO regression for variable selection, followed by logistic regression to build a parsimonious clinical score) to model the binary outcome of 'conventional therapy failure' at 6 months (defined as failure to achieve ASAS40 and ≥50% reduction in MRI SPARCC sacroiliac joint score). The model will be trained on SPACE data and validated in DESIR. Candidate predictors include demographic, clinical (BASDAI domains, duration, peripheral involvement), imaging (MRI inflammation extent), and laboratory (CRP, HLA-B27) variables available in routine care.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical decision-support tool for rheumatologists to personalize initial treatment strategy in axSpA, moving beyond a universal step-up approach. It will generate evidence for a stratified care paradigm, potentially improving long-term outcomes by preventing delayed control of inflammation. The findings will directly inform future clinical trial design and treatment guideline updates.
proposals.novelty: While prognostic factors are known, pre-2026 there is no validated, integrated risk score designed specifically to guide the initial csDMARD versus bDMARD choice in axSpA. This proposal uniquely leverages detailed, prospective early cohort data to build a clinically actionable tool for this precise decision point, incorporating both patient-reported outcomes and objective MRI metrics.

proposals.title: Personalizing Advanced Therapy Selection in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Proof-of-Concept N-of-1 Trial Series in bDMARD-Inadequate Responders
proposals.motivation: Despite multiple approved advanced therapies (TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), treatment selection remains largely trial-and-error, leading to prolonged periods of active disease and patient dissatisfaction. Current treat-to-target paradigms lack a rigorous, data-driven method to identify the optimal drug and sequence for an individual patient who has failed an initial biologic DMARD, a common clinical scenario.
proposals.hypothesis: For individual patients with active axSpA and an inadequate response to a first biologic DMARD, a structured N-of-1 trial comparing two alternative advanced therapies (e.g., a second TNFi vs. an IL-17 inhibitor) can identify a superior treatment with greater precision and patient confidence than standard sequential monotherapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of randomized, double-blind, crossover N-of-1 trials in 20-30 axSpA patients meeting the ASAS criteria with an inadequate response to a first TNFi. Each patient will undergo two treatment periods (e.g., secukinumab vs. upadacitinib), each lasting 12-16 weeks, separated by a washout/return-to-baseline period. The primary outcome will be the patient-specific treatment effect on ASDAS-CRP. Analysis will use a Bayesian hierarchical model (e.g., using R/Stan) to estimate individual treatment effects while borrowing strength across the cohort, with pre-specified decision rules (e.g., >95% probability of superiority for a clinically meaningful difference) to recommend a long-term therapy.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a formal methodological framework for personalized therapeutic decision-making in rheumatology, moving beyond population-level evidence. It will generate preliminary efficacy and feasibility data on using N-of-1 designs to guide second-line therapy in axSpA, potentially reducing the time to effective control. The results will inform the development of larger, multi-drug N-of-1 platform trials.
proposals.novelty: While N-of-1 trials are established in chronic pain and psychology, their application to compare high-cost, targeted biologic/JAK inhibitor therapies in axSpA is novel. The design directly addresses the clinical dilemma of choosing between mechanistically distinct drug classes after first TNFi failure, a common but poorly evidence-based decision point.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial of IL-17/IL-23 Pathway Inhibition Versus TNF Inhibition as First-Line Biologic Therapy in Axial Spondyloarthritis Patients with High Baseline MRI Inflammation and Elevated CRP
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), TNF inhibitors (TNFi) are the established first-line biologics, but a significant proportion of patients experience primary or secondary failure. Pre-2026 data shows non-TNF biologics (e.g., IL-17 inhibitors like secukinumab/ixekizumab) are effective, particularly in TNFi-inadequate responders. However, the optimal initial biologic choice for specific patient phenotypes remains undefined, potentially leading to delayed effective control.
proposals.hypothesis: In biologic-naïve axSpA patients with a phenotype characterized by high baseline MRI inflammation (SPARCC score ≥5) and elevated CRP (>10 mg/L), initiating treatment with an IL-17 inhibitor will lead to superior clinical and imaging outcomes at one year compared to initiating treatment with a TNF inhibitor.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, open-label, assessor-blinded, phase 4 trial. Approximately 300 biologic-naïve axSpA patients meeting ASAS criteria, with active disease (BASDAI ≥4), high MRI inflammation (SPARCC SIJ score ≥5), and elevated CRP (>10 mg/L) will be randomized 1:1 to receive either an IL-17 inhibitor (secukinumab) or a TNF inhibitor (adalimumab). The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving ASAS40 at week 52. Key secondary endpoints include change in MRI SPARCC score, ASDAS-CRP inactive disease, and time to first flare. Statistical analysis will use a logistic regression model for the primary endpoint, adjusting for center.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a phenotype-driven, inverted treatment sequence in axSpA. It could establish a new first-line biologic paradigm for a defined patient subset, potentially improving long-term outcomes and reducing the time spent on ineffective therapy. The results will inform future treatment guidelines and precision medicine approaches in rheumatology.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the entrenched treatment hierarchy by testing non-TNF biologics *before* TNFi in a targeted population, rather than after failure. It moves beyond post-hoc analyses to a prospective test of a precision medicine strategy based on pre-treatment biomarkers (MRI, CRP).

proposals.title: Defining Patient-Centric Treatment Targets in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Prospective Cohort Study Comparing Remission, Low Disease Activity, and Functional Recovery
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), remission (e.g., ASDAS-ID) is the primary treat-to-target goal, but its attainment remains challenging, especially in bDMARD-inadequate responders. Clinical experience and patient-reported outcomes suggest that some patients in low disease activity (LDA) states achieve meaningful functional recovery and quality of life, questioning the universal primacy of remission. This is critical as treatment strategies, including dose tapering as studied in RA, hinge on target definitions.
proposals.hypothesis: For a significant subset of patients with active axSpA, particularly those with prior inadequate response to bDMARDs, a composite target incorporating functional recovery (e.g., BASFI ≤ 2.0) and patient-acceptable symptom state (PASS) is more strongly associated with long-term work participation and well-being than remission defined by disease activity scores alone.
proposals.method: We propose a 2-year, prospective, observational cohort study within existing axSpA registries (e.g., leveraging SPACE cohort infrastructure). Patients initiating or switching to advanced therapies (JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib, or TNF inhibitors) will be stratified by prior bDMARD response. Primary analysis will compare three target states at 12 months: 1) ASDAS-ID remission, 2) ASDAS-LDA, 3) a novel composite of ASDAS < 2.1 + BASFI ≤ 2.0 + PASS achieved. Associations between these states and 24-month outcomes (functional disability, work productivity, pain, and radiographic progression) will be assessed using multivariable logistic and linear regression, adjusting for baseline confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence to refine treat-to-target strategies in axSpA, moving towards a more personalized framework. It will inform clinical trial endpoint selection and guideline development by quantifying the value of functional recovery as a co-primary target. The results could support more flexible treatment paradigms, optimizing resource use and patient satisfaction.
proposals.novelty: While remission is well-studied, this proposal directly challenges its sole supremacy by prospectively validating an alternative, function-centric composite target in a high-need population (bDMARD-IR). It integrates real-world registry methodology with a patient-centered outcome (PASS) not routinely used as a formal treatment target.

proposals.title: Systematic Update of the ASAS-EULAR Recommendations for the Management of Axial Spondyloarthritis: Integrating New Evidence from JAK Inhibitors and Real-World Treatment Strategies
proposals.motivation: The management landscape for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is rapidly evolving with the approval of new drug classes like JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib) and accumulating real-world evidence on treatment optimization strategies, including dose tapering. The current ASAS-EULAR recommendations (last updated in 2022) require a formal, evidence-based update to integrate this new knowledge and ensure clinical guidelines reflect the most effective and safe therapeutic options for patients, including those with an inadequate response to biologic DMARDs.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic review and synthesis of evidence from clinical trials and real-world studies published since the last guideline update will identify significant new data that necessitates changes to the existing recommendations for pharmacological management, particularly regarding the positioning of JAK inhibitors and strategies for TNF inhibitor use.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic literature review (SLR) following Cochrane methodology for studies from 2022 to 2025. The SLR will focus on phase 3/4 trials of JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, filgotinib) and real-world evidence on TNFi tapering/withdrawal and sequencing. A multidisciplinary task force, including patient research partners, will assess the evidence using the GRADE approach. Draft updated recommendations will be formulated, subjected to multiple Delphi voting rounds, and consensus will be defined as ≥80% agreement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an authoritative, evidence-based update to the primary international management guidelines for axSpA, directly impacting clinical practice. It will offer clear guidance on the use of novel therapies and treatment optimization, aiming to improve patient outcomes. The process will also establish a formal framework for more frequent, efficient guideline updates in rheumatology.
proposals.novelty: While guideline updates are common, this proposal specifically addresses the critical integration of JAK inhibitor efficacy/safety data and pragmatic real-world evidence on TNFi modulation into the core axSpA recommendations, a synthesis not yet formally undertaken. It moves beyond a simple literature review by employing a structured consensus process with pre-2026 methodologies to translate new evidence into actionable clinical guidance.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Active-Comparator Trial of Adalimumab versus Upadacitinib in Biologic-Experienced Patients with Active Ankylosing Spondylitis: The AS-SWITCH Study
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, the efficacy of TNF inhibitors like adalimumab in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is well-established, but a significant proportion of patients have an inadequate response or lose response over time. With the recent approval of JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib for active ankylosing spondylitis (AS), there is a critical clinical need for head-to-head evidence to guide treatment sequencing after first biologic failure, moving beyond placebo-controlled designs which may no longer be ethical for this patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with active AS and an inadequate response to a prior TNF inhibitor, upadacitinib is superior to adalimumab in achieving the ASAS40 response at Week 16, with a comparable safety profile.
proposals.method: This will be a Phase 3b, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, double-dummy, active-comparator trial. Approximately 400 biologic-experienced patients with active AS (fulfilling ASAS criteria, BASDAI ≥4, spinal pain ≥4) will be randomized 1:1 to receive either upadacitinib 15 mg once daily plus placebo subcutaneous injections or adalimumab 40 mg every other week plus placebo oral tablets. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving an ASAS40 response at Week 16. Key secondary endpoints include change in BASDAI, ASDAS, and safety assessments up to 52 weeks. Statistical analysis will use a mixed-effects model for repeated measures (MMRM) for continuous outcomes and logistic regression for binary outcomes, with non-inferiority and superiority testing hierarchies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level, head-to-head evidence comparing a JAK inhibitor to a TNF inhibitor in the crucial biologic-experienced AS population. It will directly inform EULAR/ASAS management guidelines on optimal second-line therapy, potentially establishing a new standard of care. The results will also set a methodological precedent for future active-comparator trials in axSpA.
proposals.novelty: While prior trials (like the referenced upadacitinib study) used placebo controls in bDMARD-inadequate responders, this design directly addresses the next logical clinical question by pitting two active, guideline-recommended but mechanistically distinct agents against each other in a blinded fashion, a comparison not yet performed in AS.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Safety of Reduced-Dose Adalimumab for Maintaining Remission in Early Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Randomised, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial
proposals.motivation: TNF inhibitors like adalimumab are highly effective for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), but their long-term use at standard doses raises concerns about cost, cumulative toxicity, and patient burden. In rheumatoid arthritis, dose tapering after achieving remission is an active research area, but robust evidence for dose reduction strategies in early axSpA, particularly with adalimumab, is lacking. This is crucial for optimizing long-term management in a young patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with early axSpA who have achieved sustained remission (ASDAS < 1.3) on standard-dose adalimumab, a reduced-dose regimen (e.g., 40 mg every 3 or 4 weeks) is non-inferior to the standard 40 mg every 2 weeks regimen for maintaining remission over 12 months.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomised, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Patients with early axSpA (fulfilling ASAS criteria, symptom duration <5 years) who have maintained ASDAS remission for ≥6 months on adalimumab 40mg every 2 weeks will be randomised 1:1:1 to continue standard dosing, 40mg every 3 weeks, or 40mg every 4 weeks. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients maintaining ASDAS inactive disease (<1.3) at 12 months, with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 15%. Secondary endpoints include flares (ASDAS ≥2.1), patient-reported outcomes (pain, BASDAI), safety, and drug levels/anti-drug antibodies. Analysis will be per-protocol and intention-to-treat.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a structured dose-reduction strategy with adalimumab in early axSpA. It will inform clinical guidelines on cost-effective and potentially safer long-term biologic use. The results could directly impact treatment paradigms, moving from indefinite standard dosing towards personalized, minimal effective dosing for maintenance.
proposals.novelty: While dose tapering has been explored in rheumatoid arthritis, its systematic application in early axSpA with adalimumab is novel. This trial specifically targets the maintenance phase in a well-defined early-disease population, a distinct clinical scenario from studying induction or withdrawal in established disease.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Efficacy-Safety Profile in AxSpA: A Randomized, Dose-Finding Study of Upadacitinib in Patients with an Inadequate Response to Biologic DMARDs
proposals.motivation: Janus kinase inhibitors like upadacitinib are effective in axial spondyloarthritis (AxSpA), but the optimal dosing to balance sustained efficacy, particularly in difficult-to-treat populations, and long-term safety remains undefined. Given the chronic nature of AxSpA and the known dose-dependent risks of JAK inhibitors, a precise understanding of the dose-response relationship is crucial for personalized treatment strategies and improved patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with active AxSpA and prior inadequate response to biologic DMARDs, a 75 mg daily dose of upadacitinib will demonstrate non-inferior efficacy to the approved 150 mg dose at one year, while offering a superior safety and tolerability profile.
proposals.method: We propose a Phase 3b, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, parallel-group, non-inferiority trial. Approximately 450 bDMARD-inadequate responder patients with active AxSpA will be randomized 1:1:1 to receive upadacitinib 75 mg, 150 mg, or a placebo-controlled reference arm (placebo advancing to 150 mg at Week 14). The primary endpoint will be the proportion of patients achieving ASAS40 at Week 52. Non-inferiority of 75 mg vs. 150 mg will be tested using a pre-specified margin. Key secondary endpoints include safety (adverse events, laboratory parameters), patient-reported outcomes (pain, fatigue, BASDAI), and imaging outcomes (MRI SPARCC score). Statistical analysis will follow the intention-to-treat principle.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a potentially optimized, lower-dose regimen of upadacitinib in a refractory AxSpA population, directly informing clinical practice. It will generate a comprehensive dataset on the comparative efficacy-safety trade-off, aiding rheumatologists and patients in shared decision-making and potentially improving long-term treatment adherence and safety.
proposals.novelty: While dose reduction studies exist for TNF inhibitors, this is a novel, prospectively designed dose-finding trial for a JAK inhibitor in AxSpA, specifically targeting the bDMARD-refractory population where the need for effective and safe options is highest, moving beyond the standard approved doses.

proposals.title: An Adaptive, Response-Guided Trial Design for Secukinumab in Axial Spondyloarthritis: Balancing Long-Term Efficacy and Safety Assessment with Ethical Treatment Delivery
proposals.motivation: Long-term, placebo-controlled trials in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) face an ethical dilemma: maintaining patients on ineffective therapy to obtain robust comparative data on safety and sustained efficacy is increasingly unacceptable. This is particularly relevant for IL-17 inhibitors like secukinumab, where long-term data beyond initial registration trials are needed. An adaptive design, inspired by oncology, is required to resolve this conflict.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a response-guided randomization design with mandatory crossover triggers in a long-term secukinumab trial will yield scientifically valid, comparative 2-year efficacy and safety data while ethically ensuring no participant remains on ineffective placebo therapy beyond a pre-defined lack-of-response milestone.
proposals.method: We propose a Phase 3b/4, randomized, double-blind, adaptive trial in patients with active axSpA. After a 16-week initial placebo-controlled period, patients not achieving an ASAS40 response will mandatorily cross over to active secukinumab treatment. A second, pre-specified interim analysis at Week 52 will assess the primary long-term endpoint (e.g., radiographic progression, BASDAI). If pre-defined efficacy criteria are met, all remaining placebo patients will cross over to active treatment. The statistical analysis plan will use a rank-preserving structural failure time model or similar methodology to adjust for the crossover effect and estimate the long-term treatment effect of continuous secukinumab versus a hypothetical placebo.
