proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, ethical framework for conducting long-term trials in chronic inflammatory diseases, generating the robust comparative data required by regulators and clinicians. It will deliver specific, long-term (104-week) efficacy and safety data for secukinumab in axSpA, directly informing treatment guidelines and patient care.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive designs exist, the formal application of an oncology-style response-guided crossover framework with specific, protocol-mandated triggers to a chronic inflammatory disease trial is novel. It directly challenges the entrenched assumption that long-term placebo arms are a necessary component of rigorous evidence generation in rheumatology.

proposals.title: Discovery of IL-17-Driven Digital Motion Phenotypes to Guide Indication Expansion for Secukinumab in Undifferentiated Axial Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of IL-17 inhibitors like secukinumab in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), identifying responsive patients in conditions with overlapping symptoms, such as non-radiographic axSpA or inflammatory back pain of short duration, remains challenging. Current clinical measures (BASDAI, ASDAS) are subjective and episodic, limiting objective phenotyping. Pre-2026, wearable sensors offer new potential for continuous, objective monitoring of disease activity, but their use for discovering drug-specific response signatures is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: That continuous motion data from wearable sensors contains distinct, quantifiable digital phenotypes specific to IL-17-mediated inflammation, and these signatures can objectively identify patients with undifferentiated axial inflammation who are most likely to respond to secukinumab therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational study using multi-sensor wearable devices (e.g., wrist, lumbar) to collect high-resolution accelerometry and gyroscope data from three cohorts: 1) AS patients initiating secukinumab, 2) nr-axSpA patients, and 3) chronic back pain patients from the SPACE cohort. We will apply pre-2026 machine learning methods, including random forest classifiers and hidden Markov models, to analyze movement patterns, nocturnal restlessness, and activity transitions. The derived features will be correlated with CRP and patient-reported outcomes to identify IL-17-specific digital biomarkers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first objective, digital biomarker signature for IL-17 pathway activity, moving beyond subjective scores. It will provide a data-driven framework for patient stratification in future proof-of-concept trials for secukinumab in novel indications, using continuous digital metrics as potential primary endpoints to de-risk clinical development.
proposals.novelty: While wearables have been explored for activity monitoring in rheumatology, this proposal uniquely aims to discover a *drug mechanism-specific* digital phenotype, using it not for monitoring but for indication-finding and trial enrichment, a novel application of digital biomarkers in pre-2026 rheumatology research.

proposals.title: A randomised controlled trial of watchful waiting versus immediate csDMARD therapy in patients with very early inflammatory arthritis: the WAIT-ERA study
proposals.motivation: Current EULAR recommendations advocate for immediate DMARD initiation in rheumatoid arthritis to prevent joint damage. However, in very early disease, a significant proportion of patients may experience self-limiting or undifferentiated arthritis. Unnecessary immediate treatment exposes these patients to potential side effects and healthcare costs without clear benefit, a concern highlighted in debates on TNF-inhibitor tapering in remission. A formal test of a delayed treatment strategy in a well-defined, early cohort is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with confirmed very early inflammatory arthritis (symptom duration <3 months), a strategy of 12-week watchful waiting with optimized NSAID/analgesia and close monitoring is non-inferior to immediate initiation of conventional synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs) with regard to the proportion of patients achieving sustained DMARD-free remission at 52 weeks.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-center, randomised, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Patients with clinically confirmed synovitis in ≥1 joint, symptom duration <12 weeks, and fulfilling the 2010 ACR/EULAR RA criteria at a low threshold (score ≥6) will be randomised 1:1 to immediate csDMARD therapy per local protocol or a watchful waiting arm. The watchful waiting protocol involves structured monitoring every 4 weeks; csDMARDs are initiated only if arthritis persists or worsens (defined by a pre-specified increase in swollen joint count and patient/physician global assessment). The primary endpoint is DMARD-free sustained remission (Boolean remission off csDMARDs for ≥24 weeks) at week 52. A non-inferiority margin of 15% will be used, with sample size calculated accordingly. Secondary endpoints include radiographic progression (Sharp/van der Heijde score), functional status (HAQ), and patient-reported pain.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from an RCT on the feasibility and outcomes of a delayed-treatment strategy in very early arthritis. It will inform clinical guidelines by potentially identifying a subgroup of patients who can avoid immediate DMARDs without harm, thereby personalizing treatment approaches. The results will also contribute to understanding the natural history of early inflammatory arthritis.
proposals.novelty: While studies like the SPACE cohort have focused on diagnostic challenges in axial SpA, and trials have tested treatment withdrawal in established remission, this proposal directly challenges the therapeutic paradigm of immediate intervention at the point of diagnosis in peripheral arthritis. It applies a 'watchful waiting' concept, common in other specialties, to a rheumatology context where it is currently considered contraindicated.

proposals.title: Optimizing Monitoring Frequency and Composite Measures for Early Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Prevent Flares and Sustain Remission
proposals.motivation: In early axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), achieving remission is a key goal with advanced therapies (e.g., TNFi, JAKi like upadacitinib). However, optimal monitoring strategies post-remission are undefined, risking overtreatment or undetected flares. Current practice varies widely, and the recent focus on tapering TNFi in RA highlights the critical need for evidence-based, disease-specific monitoring protocols to maintain long-term control.
proposals.hypothesis: A patient-tailored, algorithmic monitoring strategy using a novel composite endpoint (incorporating ASDAS, pain, and patient-reported function) with less frequent assessments (e.g., 6-monthly) is non-inferior to standard 3-monthly monitoring using conventional measures (ASDAS-CRP alone) in sustaining remission and preventing disease flares over 18 months in early axSpA patients.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Early axSpA patients (from cohorts like SPACE) in sustained remission (ASDAS <1.3) for ≥6 months on stable b/tsDMARD therapy (e.g., from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer, Janssen, Lilly) will be randomized 1:1 to Algorithm-Guided (AG) monitoring (visits at 0, 6, 12, 18 months with a predefined composite decision rule) or Standard (ST) monitoring (visits every 3 months). The primary outcome is the proportion of patients without a disease flare (ASDAS ≥2.1) at 18 months. Secondary outcomes include cost-effectiveness, patient satisfaction, and radiographic progression. Statistical analysis will follow a pre-specified non-inferiority margin.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a structured, efficient monitoring schedule in early axSpA remission, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will define a potentially superior composite measure for clinical decision-making that integrates key patient-centric outcomes, moving beyond single metrics like ASDAS.
proposals.novelty: While monitoring frequency has been studied in RA, this is the first trial to test a de-escalated, algorithm-driven monitoring strategy specifically in early axSpA, a population where early, tight control is paramount but resource use must be optimized.

proposals.title: Impact of Early Intensive Treatment with Biologic and Targeted Synthetic DMARDs on 10-Year Structural Progression and Patient-Reported Outcomes in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Prospective, Observational Analysis of the SPACE and DESIR Cohorts
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), the 'window of opportunity' hypothesis suggests early, aggressive treatment may alter long-term disease course, but evidence remains limited compared to rheumatoid arthritis. While TNF inhibitors (e.g., from AbbVie, Pfizer, Novartis) and newer agents like JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib from AbbVie) are effective, their long-term impact on disability and structural damage when initiated early is unclear. Defining optimal early treatment strategies is critical to prevent irreversible damage and improve lifelong quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with early axSpA (symptom duration <2 years) who receive intensive treatment (combination therapy or rapid escalation to biologic/targeted synthetic DMARDs) within the first year of diagnosis will have significantly lower 10-year radiographic spinal progression (mSASSS) and better long-term scores for function (BASFI), quality of life (ASAS-HI), and work productivity compared to those managed with conventional step-up therapy.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, observational study leveraging two well-characterized early axSpA cohorts: the SPACE cohort (Europe/US) and the DESIR cohort (France). We will define 'intensive early management' as initiation of a biologic (TNFi, IL-17i) or tsDMARD (JAK inhibitor) within 12 months of diagnosis, per ASAS-EULAR guidelines. Primary outcome is change in mSASSS at 10 years. Secondary outcomes include BASFI, ASAS-HI, and ASDAS. We will use propensity score matching to adjust for baseline confounders (age, sex, baseline ASDAS, CRP, smoking) and employ linear mixed-effects models for longitudinal analysis of progression. A pre-specified subgroup analysis will compare outcomes between different drug classes (TNFi vs. IL-17i vs. JAKi).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term (10-year) real-world evidence on the impact of early intensive treatment strategies on structural outcomes in axSpA. It will inform clinical guidelines on whether a 'treat-to-target' approach with early biologic/tsDMARD use is justified to prevent disability. The findings will offer crucial data for health economic evaluations and shared decision-making with patients regarding early aggressive therapy.
proposals.novelty: While SPACE has diagnosed early disease, no study has yet linked its treatment data to 10-year structural outcomes. This proposal uniquely integrates detailed early treatment intensity data from two major inception cohorts with long-term radiographic and PRO follow-up, directly testing the axSpA 'window of opportunity' hypothesis in the era of multiple advanced therapies.

proposals.title: Predicting Radiographic Progression and Treatment Response in Early Axial Spondyloarthritis Using Machine Learning Models Adapted from Oncology
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), predicting which patients with early disease will progress to radiographic damage or require advanced therapies (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) remains a major clinical challenge. This leads to delayed, often suboptimal treatment. In oncology, machine learning (ML) models successfully integrate complex, multi-modal data (clinical, imaging, lab) for precise prognostication, a methodology not yet systematically applied to early inflammatory arthritis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ML algorithms (e.g., random survival forests, gradient boosting) originally developed for cancer outcome prediction can be adapted to accurately model disease trajectories in early axSpA, identifying patients at high risk for rapid radiographic progression or inadequate response to first-line biologic therapy.
proposals.method: We will leverage the SPACE cohort and other pre-2026 axSpA trial/registry data (e.g., from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer). We will adapt and train models like random survival forests and XGBoost, which handle censored time-to-event data common in oncology trials, to predict two primary endpoints: 1) radiographic progression (mSASSS) over 2-4 years, and 2) failure to achieve ASAS40 response after 12 months of biologic DMARD therapy (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, upadacitinib). Input features will include clinical (BASDAI, ASDAS), demographic, genetic (HLA-B27), and early MRI inflammation scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven tool to stratify patients with early axSpA by prognostic risk, directly informing treat-to-target strategies and trial design. It will establish a methodological framework for translating ML approaches from oncology to rheumatology, moving beyond traditional regression models to improve personalized treatment decisions.
proposals.novelty: While ML is used in rheumatology, this proposal uniquely adapts specific algorithms and prognostic modeling philosophies from oncology—a field with more mature predictive analytics—to the specific problem of early axSpA trajectory, a direct extension of Prof. van der Heijde's work on early diagnosis (SPACE) and treatment optimization.

proposals.title: Development and Pilot Testing of a Shared Decision-Making Aid for Early Axial Spondyloarthritis: Adapting Tools from Primary Care Chronic Disease Management
proposals.motivation: Early diagnosis and treatment of axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) are crucial to prevent structural damage and disability, yet patients face complex initial treatment choices involving NSAIDs, conventional synthetic DMARDs, and increasingly, b/tsDMARDs. Current clinical practice, as highlighted in trials like the SPACE cohort, often lacks structured tools to support patient involvement in these high-stakes decisions, potentially impacting adherence and long-term outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A shared decision-making (SDM) aid, adapted from validated tools used in primary care for chronic conditions like diabetes and cardiovascular disease, will improve patient knowledge, decisional conflict, and satisfaction with the treatment plan in early axSpA compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods study. First, a multidisciplinary panel (rheumatologists, SDM experts, patients) will adapt existing primary care SDM aids (e.g., the Ottawa Personal Decision Guide) for early axSpA, incorporating pre-2026 evidence on treatment efficacy (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) and safety. The resulting tool will be a structured, paper-based booklet with visual aids. Second, we will perform a pilot randomized controlled trial (RCT) in early axSpA patients (ASAS criteria, symptom duration <2 years) from the SPONDYLOnet registry, comparing the SDM aid plus clinician training (intervention) to usual care (control), with primary outcomes being the Decisional Conflict Scale and the Shared Decision Making Questionnaire (SDM-Q-9) at 3 months.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, context-specific SDM aid for early axSpA, directly addressing the need for patient-centered care in the era of multiple advanced therapeutic options. It will generate pilot data on feasibility and impact on decision quality to power a definitive multicenter trial.
proposals.novelty: While SDM is discussed in rheumatology, the systematic adaptation of proven tools from primary care chronic disease management to the specific, high-stakes context of early axSpA treatment initiation is novel and leverages cross-disciplinary methodological expertise.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Early TNF Inhibitor Initiation versus an NSAID-First Strategy in Patients with Early Axial Spondyloarthritis: The EARLY-AXIS Study
proposals.motivation: Current ASAS/EULAR management guidelines for axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) recommend NSAIDs as first-line therapy, with bDMARDs reserved for patients with persistently high disease activity despite NSAIDs. This NSAID-first paradigm, established in the era of TNF inhibitors (TNFi), has not been prospectively tested against a strategy of immediate bDMARD initiation in early disease. Given the known link between early inflammation and subsequent structural damage, and the proven efficacy of TNFis (e.g., adalimumab, secukinumab) and JAK inhibitors (e.g., upadacitinib) in active disease, it is critical to determine if delaying potent anti-inflammatory therapy compromises long-term radiographic and functional outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with newly diagnosed, active axial spondyloarthritis, immediate initiation of a TNF inhibitor (adalimumab) will be superior to a conventional NSAID-first strategy in achieving sustained clinical remission and preventing radiographic progression over two years.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, randomized, open-label, parallel-group, superiority trial. Patients meeting ASAS classification criteria for axial SpA with symptom duration <3 years, BASDAI ≥4, and objective inflammation (MRI or CRP) will be randomized 1:1 to Arm A (immediate adalimumab 40 mg every other week) or Arm B (a protocolized NSAID-first strategy with escalation to adalimumab only if ASDAS-CRP ≥2.1 at week 12). The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving ASDAS inactive disease (<1.3) at week 104. Key secondary endpoints include change in MRI SPARCC sacroiliac joint score, mSASSS progression, and functional status (BASFI). Statistical analysis will use a modified ITT approach with logistic regression for the primary endpoint, adjusting for baseline CRP and MRI status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from an RCT to either validate or challenge the central role of NSAIDs as first-line therapy in axSpA. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on treatment sequencing. The results could shift the therapeutic paradigm towards earlier, more aggressive intervention to alter the disease course, similar to the evolution seen in rheumatoid arthritis.
proposals.novelty: While trials like RAPID-axSpA established TNFi efficacy, and observational studies (e.g., from the SPACE cohort) highlight diagnostic challenges, no study has prospectively compared immediate bDMARD use against the standard NSAID-escalation approach in early axSpA. This directly tests a foundational, yet unproven, assumption of current management.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Sulfasalazine and Leflunomide Combination Therapy in Biologic-Naïve Axial Spondyloarthritis Patients with Peripheral Synovitis
proposals.motivation: Current ASAS/EULAR guidelines, based on historical trials, universally advise against csDMARDs for axial manifestations of SpA, focusing therapy on NSAIDs and biologics. However, many patients present with a mixed phenotype including peripheral synovitis, and real-world data suggests some rheumatologists empirically use csDMARDs in this subgroup. A formal, stratified evaluation is lacking, leaving a potential, cost-effective treatment avenue unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of axial SpA patients with active peripheral synovitis will demonstrate a clinically meaningful reduction in overall disease activity, including axial pain and inflammation measured by MRI, when treated with a combination of sulfasalazine and leflunomide, compared to placebo.
proposals.method: We propose a 52-week, multicenter, double-blind, randomized controlled trial. Biologic-naïve axSpA patients (ASAS criteria) with active peripheral synovitis (≥2 swollen joints) will be randomized 1:1 to receive stable combination csDMARD therapy (sulfasalazine + leflunomide) or matched placebo, on a background of stable NSAID. The primary endpoint is the proportion achieving ASAS40 at week 24. Secondary endpoints include ASDAS-CRP, SPARCC MRI scores for spine/sacroiliac joints, swollen joint count, and patient-reported outcomes (BASDAI, pain). Pre-specified subgroup analyses will examine predictors of response, including baseline CRP, HLA-B27 status, and synovitis burden.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence to either challenge or reinforce the universal exclusion of csDMARDs in axial SpA. It will define a potential treatable trait (peripheral synovitis) and offer a structured therapeutic strategy for a patient subset, potentially delaying or reducing the need for biologic therapy. The results could directly inform future guideline updates.
proposals.novelty: This directly tests the blanket guideline recommendation in a mechanistically selected population, moving beyond historical trials that examined csDMARDs for purely axial disease. The use of a combination csDMARD regimen and MRI-based axial outcomes in this context is novel.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Risk Calculator to Guide Biologic DMARD Initiation in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A 'Treat-to-Prevent' Strategy
proposals.motivation: Current treatment paradigms in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) and rheumatoid arthritis (RA) often reserve biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs) for patients with high disease activity, despite evidence that subclinical inflammation can lead to irreversible structural damage and disability. This reactive approach mirrors outdated cardiology strategies that treated only severe hypertension, missing the window to prevent end-organ damage. A paradigm shift towards preventing long-term outcomes by treating based on future risk, rather than current severity, is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A validated, multivariable risk calculator can accurately predict the 5-year risk of radiographic progression and functional decline in patients with moderate axSpA disease activity. Using this calculator to guide bDMARD initiation in moderate-risk patients will be superior to the conventional strategy (treating only high disease activity) in preventing long-term structural damage and disability.
proposals.method: First, we will develop a 'Spondyloarthritis Risk Evaluation (SPARE)' calculator using data from the SPACE cohort and other pre-2026 axSpA cohorts (e.g., DESIR). We will employ Cox proportional hazards regression to identify independent predictors (e.g., CRP, MRI inflammation, smoking, baseline damage, HLA-B27 status) of the composite endpoint of ≥2 mSASSS units progression or loss of function (BASFI worsening). Second, we will conduct a 2-year, multicenter, randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing the SPARE-guided strategy (initiate bDMARD if 5-year risk ≥20%) versus conventional treatment (initiate per ASAS guidelines) in axSpA patients with moderate disease activity (BASDAI 4-7, ASDAS-CRP <2.1). The primary endpoint will be radiographic progression (mSASSS) at 2 years.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated tool to quantify future risk in axSpA, moving treatment decisions from a subjective assessment of current activity to an objective, personalized risk estimate. The RCT will generate high-level evidence to potentially redefine treatment thresholds in international guidelines (ASAS/EULAR), advocating for a preventative, rather than reactive, treatment model.
proposals.novelty: This directly transposes the successful 'treat-to-prevent' model from cardiology to rheumatology, a conceptual leap not yet attempted. While Dr. van der Heijde's prior work on tapering addresses de-escalation, this proposal fundamentally redefines the initial escalation threshold based on calculated future risk, not just present state.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Upadacitinib versus Placebo in Patients with Axial Spondyloarthritis and Moderate Disease Activity: The MODERATE-AxSpA Study
proposals.motivation: Current ASAS-EULAR and national guidelines recommend initiating biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs) or targeted synthetic DMARDs (tsDMARDs) like JAK inhibitors primarily in patients with persistently high disease activity despite NSAID therapy. This leaves a significant population with moderate disease activity at risk of structural progression and functional decline while on conventional therapy. Given the proven efficacy of agents like upadacitinib (AbbVie) and secukinumab (Novartis) in high-activity populations, it is critical to determine if earlier intervention at a moderate disease activity threshold can better prevent long-term radiographic damage and disability, potentially altering the treatment paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: Initiating advanced therapy (upadacitinib) in patients with axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) and moderate disease activity, as defined by an Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score (ASDAS) between 2.1 and 3.5, will result in superior inhibition of radiographic spinal progression at 2 years and greater attainment of clinical remission compared to a treat-to-target strategy with conventional therapy alone.
proposals.method: This will be a 2-year, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Approximately 300 biologic-naïve patients with active axSpA (fulfilling ASAS criteria) and moderate disease activity (ASDAS 2.1-3.5) will be randomized 1:1 to receive upadacitinib 15mg daily or matched placebo, both on a background of optimized NSAIDs. The primary endpoint will be the change in modified Stoke Ankylosing Spondylitis Spinal Score (mSASSS) from baseline to week 104, assessed by two blinded central readers. Key secondary endpoints include the proportion of patients achieving ASDAS inactive disease (<1.3) and ASAS40 response at week 52. Statistical analysis will use a linear mixed model for the primary endpoint and logistic regression for secondary endpoints, with an intention-to-treat population.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on whether a strategy of treating to a target of remission/low disease activity, starting from a state of moderate activity, is superior to the current standard of waiting for high disease activity. It will directly inform updates to international management guidelines for axSpA. The results could justify a paradigm shift towards earlier, more aggressive intervention to prevent irreversible structural damage.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. van der Heijde's prior work (e.g., SPACE cohort) focuses on early diagnosis and her recent trials (e.g., upadacitinib in bDMARD-inadequate responders) focus on later lines of therapy, this proposal uniquely tests the necessity of the 'high disease activity' threshold itself for treatment initiation, a foundational but unproven premise in current axSpA management.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Immediate Tumor Necrosis Factor Inhibitor (TNFi) versus Conventional Step-Up Therapy in Patients with Early Axial Spondyloarthritis and Poor Prognostic Factors: The TOP-DOWN AxSpA Study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), the conventional therapeutic pyramid starts with NSAIDs and delays biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs), potentially allowing irreversible structural damage to accrue in patients with severe early disease. Pre-2026 evidence from rheumatoid arthritis shows superior outcomes with early, aggressive intervention. Applying this 'top-down' paradigm to early axSpA patients with poor prognostic factors (e.g., high CRP, MRI inflammation, syndesmophytes at baseline) could fundamentally alter disease trajectory and prevent long-term disability.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with early axSpA (symptom duration <2 years) and predefined poor prognostic factors, immediate initiation of a TNF inhibitor (adalimumab or secukinumab) will lead to superior clinical and radiographic outcomes at 2 years compared to a conventional step-up strategy starting with NSAIDs.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, randomized, open-label, controlled trial. Patients from the SPACE cohort and similar early axSpA cohorts, fulfilling ASAS criteria with symptom duration <2 years and ≥2 poor prognostic factors, will be randomized 1:1 to Arm A (immediate TNFi or IL-17i) or Arm B (conventional step-up therapy). The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients achieving ASDAS-CRP inactive disease at 104 weeks. Key secondary endpoints include change in mSASSS, ASAS40 response, and patient-reported outcomes like pain and function. Statistical analysis will use a linear mixed model for repeated measures, with non-inferiority and superiority testing as pre-specified.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a 'top-down' treatment strategy in early axSpA, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will generate crucial data on whether early, aggressive suppression of inflammation in high-risk patients can halt radiographic progression, a key unmet need. The results could redefine the standard of care for a significant subset of axSpA patients, shifting the paradigm from reactive to preventive treatment.
proposals.novelty: While 'top-down' strategies are established in RA, their systematic testing in early axSpA, specifically targeting a poor-prognosis phenotype identified at diagnosis, is novel. This moves beyond studying bDMARD efficacy in established disease to testing a fundamental re-ordering of first-line therapy based on prognostic stratification.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial to Evaluate the Efficacy of a Structured, Digital Non-Pharmacological Intervention Program on Pain and Function in Patients with Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA) on Stable Biologic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Despite the proven efficacy of advanced pharmacologic therapies (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) in axSpA, many patients continue to experience residual pain, fatigue, and functional impairment. Current EULAR/ASAS management recommendations advocate for combining pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments, but the evidence base for specific, structured non-drug interventions, particularly in the era of biologics, remains limited and of low quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, digitally delivered non-pharmacological intervention program, combining personalized exercise, pain neuroscience education, and cognitive-behavioral strategies, will lead to significantly greater improvements in patient-reported pain and physical function compared to standard care alone in axSpA patients with stable but symptomatic disease on biologic DMARDs.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized, assessor-blinded, controlled trial. Patients with active axSpA (BASDAI ≥4) on stable biologic therapy for ≥3 months will be randomized 1:1 to the intervention or control (standard care). The intervention is a 12-week, app-based program with: 1) an AI-driven, adaptive exercise module (using algorithms to tailor intensity based on daily patient-reported pain and fatigue), 2) interactive pain neuroscience education modules, and 3) CBT-based activity pacing tools. The primary endpoint is the change in BASDAI at week 12. Secondary endpoints include changes in BASFI, ASAS Health Index, and objectively measured physical activity (via accelerometer). Analysis will be by intention-to-treat using mixed models for repeated measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the additive value of a specific, scalable non-pharmacological intervention in a real-world axSpA population already on advanced therapy. It will inform future EULAR treatment guidelines by defining a potentially effective, standardized adjunctive care model. The digital delivery method offers insights into implementing such programs broadly in clinical practice.
proposals.novelty: This trial moves beyond generic exercise advice to test a comprehensive, theory-based, and digitally adaptive intervention specifically designed for the residual symptom burden in biologically-treated axSpA patients, a population largely unstudied in prior non-pharmacological trials.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center, Randomized Trial Comparing Treatment Sequences of IL-17 and TNF Inhibitors in Axial Spondyloarthritis: The SPINE-SEQUENCE Study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), both TNF inhibitors (TNFi) and IL-17 inhibitors (IL-17i) are effective, but evidence-based guidance on their optimal sequencing is lacking. Current trials compare single drugs, leaving clinicians without data on which sequence (e.g., TNFi-first vs. IL-17i-first) provides the longest-lasting, tolerable disease control for patients, a critical question for chronic management. This gap mirrors historical challenges in oncology, where sequencing of targeted therapies is now systematically evaluated.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with active axSpA requiring advanced therapy, initiating treatment with an IL-17 inhibitor, followed by a TNF inhibitor upon failure, will yield a longer 'total treatment survival' than the reverse sequence (TNFi-first then IL-17i).
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, multi-center, randomized, open-label trial. Biologic-naïve patients with active axSpA will be randomized 1:1 to Sequence A (IL-17i first, switch to TNFi upon failure) or Sequence B (TNFi first, switch to IL-17i upon failure). The primary endpoint is 'total treatment survival,' defined as the cumulative time from randomization until failure of the second biologic in the sequence, where failure is a composite of inadequate efficacy (e.g., failure to achieve ASAS40 or loss of response) or intolerability. Secondary endpoints include time-to-first-treatment-failure, patient-reported outcomes (pain, function), and safety. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with Kaplan-Meier estimates and Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence directly comparing treatment sequences in axSpA, moving beyond single-drug efficacy. It will deliver a practical, patient-relevant metric ('total treatment survival') to guide long-term therapeutic strategy. The findings will inform international treatment recommendations (ASAS/EULAR) and offer a novel trial framework applicable to other rheumatic diseases.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the oncology 'head-to-head sequence' trial design to rheumatology, a novel conceptual shift. It pragmatically tests entire management pathways rather than individual drugs, addressing a core clinical dilemma not resolved by existing registration trials for agents from Novartis, Lilly, Pfizer, AbbVie, or Janssen.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness and Sequencing of IL-17 versus TNF Inhibition in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Prospective, Observational Cohort Study from the ASAS Registry
proposals.motivation: For patients with axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) requiring advanced therapy, TNF inhibitors (TNFi) are the established first-line biologic. However, IL-17 inhibitors (IL-17i) are also approved, and head-to-head randomized trials are scarce. In clinical practice, the optimal sequencing strategy—starting with TNFi versus IL-17i, or switching between classes—remains unclear, leading to potentially suboptimal patient outcomes and inefficient resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: In bio-naïve patients with active axSpA, first-line IL-17i will demonstrate non-inferior effectiveness to first-line TNFi at 12 months, as measured by ASAS40. Furthermore, in patients failing a first biologic, switching to the alternative mechanism of action will be superior to switching within the same class.
proposals.method: We will design a prospective, observational cohort study leveraging the international ASAS (Assessment of SpondyloArthritis international Society) registry. Patients initiating a first biologic (TNFi or IL-17i) or switching after inadequate response will be enrolled. The primary analysis will use propensity score matching (PSM) with inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) to adjust for baseline confounders (e.g., CRP, BASDAI, presence of psoriasis). The primary outcome is ASAS40 response at 12 months. Secondary analyses will employ multivariable logistic and Cox regression models to compare time-to-response, retention rates, and safety profiles, with a focus on identifying clinical predictors of response to each drug class.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide real-world evidence to directly inform EULAR/ASAS treatment recommendations on the positioning of IL-17i relative to TNFi. It will offer clinicians data-driven guidance on first-line biologic choice and subsequent sequencing strategies, aiming to improve long-term disease control. The analysis of predictive factors will move the field towards a more personalized treatment approach in axSpA.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have established the efficacy of both drug classes versus placebo, this proposal directly compares their real-world effectiveness and systematically evaluates sequencing strategies in a large, multinational cohort, a question not addressed by existing pivotal trials.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Trial Comparing Standardized, Personalized, and Usual-Care Treat-to-Target Strategies in Axial Spondyloarthritis: The TARGET-axSpA Study
proposals.motivation: Treat-to-target (T2T) strategies in axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) are predominantly based on standardized protocols targeting objective inflammation (e.g., ASDAS, CRP). However, patient priorities often extend beyond inflammation to pain, fatigue, and function, leading to potential discordance. With the expanding therapeutic arsenal (TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi), a critical, untested assumption is that a single T2T protocol is optimal for all patients, potentially overlooking individual treatment goals and heterogeneous disease drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: A patient-prioritized, personalized T2T strategy will be non-inferior to a standardized inflammation-targeted strategy for achieving a composite outcome of disease control and patient-reported benefit, and both will be superior to usual care. Furthermore, differential responses to these strategies can be predicted by baseline genomic, proteomic, and imaging biomarkers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month, multicenter, pragmatic, randomized controlled trial with three parallel arms: 1) Standardized T2T (target: ASDAS < 2.1), 2) Personalized T2T (target: individual patient-prioritized ASAS core domain, e.g., pain, fatigue, or function), and 3) Usual Care. The primary outcome is a 24-month composite of disease activity (ASDAS), physical function (BASFI), and Patient Global Assessment. A nested biomarker cohort (n=300) will undergo deep phenotyping at baseline and 12 months, including whole-genome sequencing, Olink proteomics (inflammation, neurology panels), and spinal MRI (Berlin scoring). We will apply supervised machine learning (e.g., random forest, elastic net regression) to identify multi-modal biomarker signatures predictive of optimal response to each strategy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence comparing personalized versus standardized T2T in axSpA, directly informing clinical guidelines. The biomarker analysis will generate a foundational dataset for precision medicine, proposing a stratification model to match patients to the T2T strategy most likely to succeed based on their molecular and clinical phenotype.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the 'one-size-fits-all' T2T paradigm by formally testing a patient-prioritized strategy against the standard. The integration of a comprehensive biomarker discovery program within a pragmatic trial design is novel and moves beyond comparing drug efficacy to optimizing treatment strategy selection.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an axSpA-Specific Cardiovascular Risk Prediction Model Using International Registry Data
proposals.motivation: Patients with axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) have a significantly increased risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD), which is not adequately captured by general population risk scores like SCORE2. This unmet clinical need is critical, as effective management of axSpA with therapies from companies like Novartis, AbbVie, and Pfizer may influence CVD risk. Pre-2026, there is no validated, integrated risk score that incorporates axSpA-specific factors such as disease activity, structural damage, and treatment exposure to guide preventive strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A cardiovascular risk prediction model that integrates traditional risk factors with axSpA-specific clinical and treatment variables will demonstrate superior discrimination and calibration compared to established general population models when applied to a large, international axSpA registry cohort.
proposals.method: We will utilize data from large, established axSpA registries (e.g., ASAS-COMOSPA, national biologic registries). Candidate predictors will include traditional CVD risk factors, axSpA-specific measures (BASDAI, ASDAS, CRP, radiographic damage), and treatment history (NSAIDs, TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, JAK inhibitors). We will employ Cox proportional hazards regression with backward selection for model development, followed by internal validation via bootstrapping and external validation in a separate registry cohort. Model performance will be assessed using C-statistics, calibration plots, and net reclassification improvement (NRI) compared to SCORE2.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated, disease-specific CVD risk calculator for axSpA, enabling rheumatologists to identify high-risk patients for targeted interventions. It will clarify the contribution of disease activity and specific drug classes (e.g., TNFi, JAKi like upadacitinib) to CVD risk, informing both rheumatologic and cardiovascular management guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While the association between axSpA and CVD is known, creating a clinically actionable prediction tool by synthesizing large-scale, real-world registry data with advanced statistical modeling is novel. This moves beyond describing risk to providing a personalized assessment tool, integrating the complex interplay of inflammation, treatment, and traditional factors.

proposals.title: A randomised, placebo-controlled trial of upadacitinib in HLA-B27-positive individuals with inflammatory back pain for the prevention of axial spondyloarthritis: the PREVENT-axSpA study
proposals.motivation: Axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) often has a long diagnostic delay, leading to irreversible structural damage. The SPACE cohort, led by this researcher, demonstrated the challenge of early diagnosis. Pre-emptive intervention in a clearly defined at-risk population (HLA-B27+ with early inflammatory back pain) could potentially alter the disease course, a concept not yet tested in axSpA but informed by research on treatment tapering in rheumatoid arthritis remission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 12-month course of upadacitinib, a Janus kinase inhibitor, in HLA-B27-positive individuals with inflammatory back pain of less than 12 months duration will be superior to placebo in preventing progression to a formal diagnosis of axial spondyloarthritis according to ASAS classification criteria.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-centre, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled phase IIb trial. Participants will be HLA-B27+ adults aged 18-45 with chronic back pain (3-12 months) of inflammatory character but not fulfilling ASAS criteria for axSpA. They will be randomised 1:1 to upadacitinib 15mg once daily or placebo for 52 weeks. The primary endpoint is the proportion of patients diagnosed with axSpA (by central adjudication using ASAS criteria) at week 52. Secondary endpoints include changes in pain (measured by VAS), MRI inflammation (SPARCC score for spine/sacroiliac joints), and patient-reported outcomes (ASAS HI, BASDAI). Statistical analysis will use a logistic regression model for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the feasibility and efficacy of ultra-early, pre-emptive intervention in axSpA. It will establish whether disease modification or even prevention is possible, potentially redefining the therapeutic paradigm. The results will inform future clinical guidelines and identify a potential therapeutic window for disease interception.
proposals.novelty: While recent trials (e.g., with upadacitinib) focus on established, active disease, this proposal tests a true prevention strategy in a pre-disease state. It directly extends the researcher's work on early diagnosis (SPACE) and treatment optimization (tapering trials) into a novel, proactive therapeutic domain.

proposals.title: Defining and Overcoming the 'Low Disease Activity Plateau' in Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Phase 3 Trials and a Prospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: In axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA), treat-to-target strategies aim for remission or inactive disease. However, a significant subset of patients on advanced therapies (e.g., TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi) achieves only a stable low disease activity (LDA) state without progressing to target, representing a therapeutic plateau. This state, poorly characterized, may carry long-term risks of structural progression and functional impairment, yet optimal management (e.g., dose escalation, switching, combination) remains undefined, leading to clinical uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with axSpA who sustain LDA without achieving remission on biologic/targeted synthetic DMARDs have distinct clinical, imaging, and biomarker profiles compared to those achieving remission, and a structured intervention (e.g., dose optimization) will increase the proportion attaining remission compared to maintaining stable therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase study. Phase 1: A post-hoc analysis of pooled patient-level data from industry-sponsored Phase 3 trials (e.g., from Novartis, AbbVie, Pfizer, Lilly, Janssen) in axSpA to define the LDA plateau population using ASDAS-LDA criteria. We will apply multivariable logistic regression and latent class analysis to identify predictors (clinical, CRP, MRI inflammation, treatment type) of being 'stuck' in LDA at weeks 52-104. Phase 2: A prospective, multicenter, randomized pragmatic trial where consenting patients identified in LDA in clinical practice are randomized to either a treat-to-target intervention arm (protocolized dose escalation or switch per ASAS/EULAR guidelines) or a stable therapy control arm, with remission (ASDAS <1.3) at 12 months as primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first operational definition and characterization of the 'LDA plateau' in axSpA, informing future trial design and guidelines. It will generate evidence for whether actively treating beyond LDA is beneficial, directly addressing a common and costly dilemma in rheumatology practice.
proposals.novelty: While research has focused on achieving response versus non-response, this proposal specifically investigates the sub-optimal outcome of persistent LDA, a clinically relevant but understudied grey zone. It uniquely leverages existing trial data to define the problem and then tests a practical solution in a real-world setting.

proposals.title: Efficacy of a Collaborative Care Model Integrating Rheumatology and Mental Health Services on Disease Activity, Pain, and Depression in Patients with Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is characterized by chronic back pain, fatigue, and high rates of depression, which are often inadequately addressed in standard rheumatologic care. Pre-2026 evidence from cohorts like SPACE confirms the early burden of disease, while trials of advanced therapies (e.g., TNFi, JAK inhibitors like upadacitinib) primarily target inflammation, leaving a significant unmet need for holistic management. Integrating mental health support is crucial for improving patient-reported outcomes and overall quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, protocol-driven collaborative care model integrating rheumatology and mental health services will lead to greater improvements in composite outcomes of disease activity (ASDAS), pain (NRS), and depressive symptoms (PHQ-9) over 12 months, compared to standard rheumatologic care alone, in patients with active axSpA.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial. Patients with active axSpA (ASDAS ≥2.1) and comorbid depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 ≥10) will be randomized 1:1 to the integrated care arm or standard care. The intervention arm will follow a manualized protocol where a dedicated care manager coordinates regular consultations between the rheumatologist and an on-site psychologist/psychiatrist, employing cognitive-behavioral therapy for pain and depression management alongside pharmacologic treatment. The primary endpoint is a binary composite of ≥20% improvement in pain NRS AND ≥50% reduction in PHQ-9 score AND ASDAS major improvement (≥2.0 decrease or score <1.3) at 12 months. Analysis will be by intention-to-treat using logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from an RCT on the effectiveness of a fully integrated biopsychosocial care model in axSpA. It will generate a replicable protocol for implementation in clinical practice and offer critical data on how addressing mental health concurrently impacts key inflammatory disease outcomes, potentially informing future treatment guidelines and healthcare policy.
proposals.novelty: While the link between mental health and axSpA is recognized, no pre-2026 RCT has tested a formal, co-located care model with a composite endpoint targeting both mental and inflammatory disease domains as a primary outcome, moving beyond observational correlations to prove interventional efficacy.

ideas.title: Applying 'Treat-to-Prevent' from Cardiovascular Disease to Rheumatology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high disease activity is a necessary threshold for initiating biologic DMARDs (bDMARDs) by drawing an analogy from cardiovascular medicine. In cardiology, the solution to preventing catastrophic events was to shift from treating only severe hypertension or cholesterol to initiating statins and antihypertensives at moderate levels based on calculated future risk. We adapt this solution by proposing to develop and validate a 'rheumatologic risk calculator' for patients with moderate disease activity. A clinical trial would then test if using this calculator to guide early bDMARD initiation prevents long-term joint damage and disability better than the conventional strategy, fundamentally redefining the treatment threshold from one of current severity to one of future risk.

ideas.title: Adaptive Trial Design with Response-Guided Randomization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the ethical assumption that long-term placebo arms are necessary for rigorous evidence by adapting a solution from oncology trials. In metastatic cancer research, it became unethical to keep patients on placebo/ineffective therapy once a treatment demonstrated survival benefit. The field solved this with adaptive designs like 'response-guided randomization,' where patients on the control arm crossover to the active treatment upon disease progression or at a pre-specified interim efficacy milestone. Applying this to secukinumab trials, we propose a modified long-term study design for chronic inflammatory diseases. The trial would start with a placebo-controlled phase but include mandatory, protocol-specified crossover triggers (e.g., lack of PASI 75 response by Week 16, or a clinically significant disease flare). This adaptation balances the scientific need for long-term comparative data on safety and sustained effect against the ethical imperative to provide effective treatment, ensuring no participant is subjected to long-term, ineffective placebo therapy once a lack of response is demonstrated.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Head-to-Head vs. Standard-of-Care' Trial Framework from Oncology to Compare IL-17i and TNFi Sequencing
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from oncology's approach to sequencing targeted therapies for metastatic cancer, this research idea adapts the 'head-to-head vs. standard-of-care sequence' trial framework. In oncology, pragmatic platform trials compare sequences using endpoints like time-to-treatment-failure to determine which order provides the most durable benefit. Adapted to rheumatology/dermatology, this would involve a multi-center trial where patients are randomized to different first-line sequencing strategies for IL-17 inhibitors (IL-17i) and TNF inhibitors (TNFi). The primary endpoint would be 'total treatment survival,' measuring the cumulative time a patient remains on effective and tolerable therapy from sequence initiation. This approach moves beyond comparing single drugs to directly evaluating which treatment sequence maximizes long-term disease control and patient quality of life, providing clear, practical guidance for clinicians.

ideas.title: Challenge remission as the universal primary treatment target

ideas.title: Digital Biomarker-Driven Indication Expansion for IL-17 Inhibitors
ideas.description: This research program integrates wearable motion sensors for continuous, objective monitoring of joint and systemic symptoms in patients with various immune-mediated diseases. The high-resolution, longitudinal digital movement data is analyzed to identify distinct, quantifiable motion signatures or 'digital phenotypes' associated with IL-17 pathway activity. These discovered digital biomarkers are then used to screen patient cohorts with conditions of unknown or mixed pathophysiology, such as non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis or specific IBD subtypes, to objectively identify those most likely to respond to IL-17 inhibition. This data-driven approach enables precision indication-finding for secukinumab, potentially accelerating and de-risking clinical trials by using continuous digital metrics as primary endpoints for proof-of-concept studies in novel diseases.

ideas.description: This study evaluates a biomarker-stratified, adaptive treatment strategy for early-stage disease with poor prognostic factors. At diagnosis, patients are risk-stratified using a rapid clinical and multi-omics prognostic panel to identify an ultra-high-risk subset with a high predicted probability of refractory progression. This enriched cohort is then randomized to immediate bDMARD therapy versus accelerated conventional step-up, while lower-risk patients follow standard protocols. The design directly tests whether a precision-guided inversion of the pyramid improves outcomes and cost-effectiveness by targeting advanced therapy to those least likely to respond to first-line treatments.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal AI for Predicting Microbial Dysbiosis Risk from Musculoskeletal Imaging in Spondyloarthritis
ideas.description: This research direction applies deep learning to radiographic images (e.g., spine and sacroiliac joint MRIs/CTs) from patients with spondyloarthritis to extract imaging biomarkers that correlate with specific gut microbiome states, particularly those induced by or predictive of vulnerability during IL-17A inhibition. By training a model on paired longitudinal imaging and microbiome sequencing data, the goal is to develop an imaging-based proxy for dysbiosis, identifying patients at high risk for complications like candidiasis before or early in therapy. This creates a clinically practical tool where a routine scan can inform personalized monitoring and prophylactic strategies, bridging radiology and gastroenterology for systemic risk prediction.

ideas.title: The Heterogeneity-Enabled Pragmatic Trial (HEPT): A Large-Scale, Embedded HTE Study of Secukinumab in Routine Practice
ideas.description: This study conducts a large-scale, pragmatic trial of secukinumab across diverse clinical settings but is fundamentally redesigned to move beyond estimating an average effect. Its core is a pre-planned, multi-layered analysis framework to rigorously investigate heterogeneity of treatment effect (HTE). The study prospectively collects high-dimensional data on biomarkers, clinical phenotypes, comorbidities, and social determinants of health, then applies machine learning and causal forest techniques to identify and validate patient subgroups with meaningfully different effectiveness and safety outcomes. The final output is not just a report on overall effectiveness, but a validated, clinically actionable stratification tool to guide personalized treatment decisions in real-world practice.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Aggressive First-Line' Oncology Model to Early Severe Rheumatic Disease
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption of the rheumatic disease treatment pyramid by drawing a direct analogy from oncology's successful paradigm shift for aggressive cancers. In fields like hematology, treating high-risk disease with the most potent available combination therapy first ('induction therapy') has improved survival and cure rates by preventing evolution of resistance and metastatic spread. The adapted research idea is to study a top-down 'induction' approach for early, severe rheumatic disease with poor prognostic factors. Patients would immediately initiate a potent, potentially multi-mechanistic bDMARD regimen as first-line therapy, bypassing traditional sequential steps. The study would evaluate if this aggressive early intervention leads to superior long-term outcomes—preventing irreversible joint damage, inducing sustained remission, and potentially allowing for simpler maintenance therapy later—thereby inverting the traditional treatment pyramid.

ideas.title: Repurposing a Core Mechanism: A Platform Approach to IL-17 Inhibition
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's use of a common core vehicle platform adapted for multiple missions, this idea proposes a 'platform' approach to scaling secukinumab across indications. The analogous solution involves standardizing a reliable core (like a rocket booster) and adapting it with mission-specific modules. Adapted to this research context, the solution is to formally establish IL-17A inhibition by secukinumab as a core therapeutic platform. A coordinated, modular clinical development program would then be designed to efficiently test this platform across multiple IL-17-mediated diseases like ankylosing spondylitis and inflammatory bowel disease. This approach uses shared core data on drug behavior and safety while allowing for indication-specific optimization, creating a systematic and efficient method for scaling across the therapeutic spectrum of a validated biological pathway.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Triage: Differentiating Biological Non-Response from Modifiable Causes in Secukinumab Therapy
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated clinical decision-support system that not only predicts a lack of response to secukinumab but also actively classifies its probable cause. The system uses longitudinal data from electronic health records, pharmacy claims, and patient-reported outcomes to differentiate true biologic refractory disease from failures due to adherence issues, diagnostic inaccuracy, or clinical complications. By providing this mechanistic triage, it guides targeted interventions—such as adherence support or re-evaluation of diagnosis—for a subset of patients, while cleanly identifying a core group of biological non-responders for subsequent mechanistic studies or alternative therapy escalation.

ideas.title: The Pragmatic Effectiveness Network (PEN) Trial
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from agricultural on-farm trials, this idea proposes to solve the scaling challenge by adapting a decentralized, real-environment testing model. The analogous solution from crop science involves testing innovations across numerous working farms under varied local conditions to assess true performance and adaptability. Adapted to the secukinumab context, this translates to conducting a large-scale, pragmatic trial through a consortium of diverse, community-based dermatology practices—a Pragmatic Effectiveness Network (PEN). Clinicians would enroll patients according to their routine clinical judgment for psoriasis treatment. The study would employ a minimal-intervention, common-data-element framework to collect key effectiveness and safety outcomes, thereby evaluating secukinumab's real-world performance across the full heterogeneity of patients, practice settings, and concomitant care observed in routine clinical practice, moving beyond the constraints of traditional clinical trial settings.

ideas.title: Dynamic Immunological Trajectory Modeling for Predicting and Optimizing IL-17A Inhibitor Response
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static baseline biomarkers to model the dynamic immunological response to IL-17A inhibition. It employs dense, longitudinal multi-omics profiling (e.g., blood transcriptomics, serum proteomics, cutaneous/synovial biomarkers, microbiome) from baseline through the first 12 weeks of secukinumab therapy. A computational framework integrates this temporal data to identify critical early on-treatment shift signatures that predict long-term outcomes. The goal is to create an adaptive prediction model that can inform early intervention or therapy adjustment, thereby addressing the inherent variability in patient trajectories and prior treatment history.

ideas.title: A Causal, Adaptive Platform Trial for Predictive Biomarker Discovery in axSpA Treatment Stratification
ideas.description: This redesigned study establishes a multi-center, adaptive platform trial for axSpA where patients are randomized to standard-of-care therapies (NSAIDs, bDMARDs). It integrates deep, longitudinal clinical phenotyping with serial high-throughput omics (e.g., proteomics, transcriptomics) collected at baseline and pre-specified early time points. Advanced analytical pipelines, incorporating causal inference methods and Bayesian machine learning, are used to identify dynamic biomarkers that are predictive of future response to a specific therapy versus another, actively controlling for confounders. The most promising biomarker signatures are then prospectively validated by guiding patient stratification in newly added trial arms, creating a closed-loop system for generating clinically actionable, causal predictors.

ideas.title: Refine cost-effectiveness models for biologics

ideas.title: Use adaptive dose-finding from Phase I oncology trials for secukinumab optimization
ideas.description: This idea applies the Bayesian adaptive dose-finding methodology pioneered in Phase I oncology trials, specifically designs like the Continual Reassessment Method (CRM), to the problem of optimizing the secukinumab dose for a new inflammatory disease. In oncology, these methods efficiently find the maximum tolerated dose by dynamically updating dose assignment probabilities based on real-time patient outcomes. Adapted here, the trial would use a pre-specified Bayesian model to continuously update the estimated dose-response and dose-safety relationships for secukinumab. Patient allocation would be skewed in real-time toward doses with the highest posterior probability of offering the best balance of high efficacy and acceptable safety, thereby requiring a smaller sample size and exposing fewer patients to suboptimal doses compared to traditional fixed, multi-arm designs.

ideas.title: Dynamic Precision Dosing: Early Identification of Non-Responders to Optimize Long-Term NSAID Regimens
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of early non-response identification and long-term regimen optimization to develop a dynamic, precision-dosing framework for NSAIDs. It aims to create predictive models that use early clinical, biomarker, and pharmacometric data to identify patients who will not achieve an adequate therapeutic response to a standard NSAID regimen. For these predicted non-responders, the model will simultaneously recommend an optimized, alternative long-term dosing strategy (e.g., dose escalation, adjunctive therapy, or switch to a different drug class) to maximize benefit while minimizing toxicity. This proactive, two-stage approach moves beyond static dosing guidelines by enabling personalized, pre-emptive regimen adjustments based on an individual's predicted response trajectory.

ideas.title: Microbiome-Guided Timing of IL-17A Inhibition: Treating Moderate Disease to Prevent Progression and Candidiasis Risk
ideas.description: This research program investigates whether initiating IL-17A inhibitors at moderate disease activity, guided by baseline gut microbiome profiles, can prevent disease progression more effectively than waiting for high activity, while also identifying microbial signatures that predict subsequent candidiasis risk. It directly tests the hypothesis that a favorable pre-treatment microbiome enables a safer and more effective early intervention strategy. The study would prospectively compare clinical and radiographic outcomes, alongside longitudinal microbiome and fungal colonization dynamics, between moderate-activity initiators and a conventional high-activity control group. This approach synergistically addresses both overtreatment concerns and adverse event management by making the microbiome a central decision-making tool for treatment timing.

ideas.title: Applying Dose-Response Landscape Mapping from Agrochemical Research to Clinical Dosing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that 150 mg and 75 mg are optimal by adapting a solution from agrochemical science. In that field, determining the optimal application rate for a herbicide involves constructing a full dose-response landscape across a wide, continuous range of concentrations to identify the precise inflection points for efficacy and toxicity. Adapted to clinical pharmacology, this means designing a study that tests a finely graduated spectrum of doses (e.g., from 10 mg to 250 mg) rather than a few predefined ones. The trial would intensively map both therapeutic and adverse response surfaces across this range, aiming to pinpoint the exact dose where efficacy plateaus and the dose where the safety profile begins to degrade, thereby empirically defining the optimal efficacy-safety window and potentially validating or refining the current standard doses.

ideas.title: Causally-Informed, Outcome-Anchored AI for Joint Damage Progression Biomarkers
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond automated scoring to discover and validate imaging biomarkers that are causally relevant to clinical disease progression. It trains deep learning models using paired longitudinal radiographic images and real-world clinical outcome data from multi-center prospective cohorts, ensuring the learned features predict future patient state. The architecture explicitly disentangles technical confounders from biological signal using adversarial networks and domain generalization techniques. Finally, the system evaluates the candidate imaging biomarkers within a causal inference framework to assess their role as mediators between treatment and outcome, aiming to produce robust, actionable tools for clinical trials and practice.

ideas.title: Applying Crop Yield Prediction Methods to AxSpA Treatment Response
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from precision agriculture, where high-throughput omics and machine learning are used to predict crop yield based on complex environmental and biological inputs. The analogous problem of predicting optimal interventions for heterogeneous systems is solved by integrating multi-source data into predictive models. Adapted to axSpA, the research would employ a similar integrative ML approach, combining patient-derived multi-omics data (e.g., gut microbiome, immunophenotyping, genomics) with detailed clinical profiles to discover biomarker signatures that predict which patients will respond to specific NSAIDs or biologic DMARDs, thereby enabling a precision medicine strategy for axSpA treatment selection.

ideas.title: Model-Informed Precision Dosing Platform to Define Covariate-Adjusted Optimal Dosing Rules
ideas.description: This study moves beyond comparing a few fixed doses to develop a precision dosing framework. It integrates rich patient data (e.g., genetics, biomarkers, disease activity scores) with pharmacometric models to simulate outcomes across a continuous dosing range for diverse virtual patient populations. The core clinical validation is an adaptive, multi-arm trial where patients are randomized to either a model-recommended personalized dose or a standard fixed dose, with the model continuously updated. The primary outcome is the superiority of the personalized dosing arm in achieving a composite efficacy-safety endpoint, thereby directly testing a dynamic dosing strategy against static benchmarks.

ideas.title: Define Personalized NSAID Dosing Schedules Using Chronopharmacology and Biomarker Feedback
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded by analogy to precision agriculture's solution for optimal irrigation. Instead of fixed watering schedules, smart irrigation uses soil sensors and weather forecasts in a closed-loop system to apply water precisely when and where it's needed. Adapting this solution, the research should establish dynamic, personalized NSAID regimens. The goal is to develop and validate dosing algorithms that integrate continuous or frequent biomarker feedback (reflecting inflammation, renal function, and GI mucosal integrity) and individual chronopharmacological data to adjust NSAID dose and administration timing in real-time. This approach aims to maximize the anti-inflammatory and analgesic benefit while preemptively minimizing toxicity by responding to early signals of stress, effectively creating a 'smart dosing' system for long-term NSAID use.

ideas.title: Adapting Structural Health Monitoring for Continuous Joint Biomechanical Assessment
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) in civil engineering to the problem of continuous joint monitoring. In SHM, networks of wireless sensors are deployed on infrastructure like bridges to continuously collect vibration and strain data, using changes in the structure's dynamic 'fingerprint' to predict failure. Adapted to a medical context, this involves using a wearable network of motion sensors (like IMUs) placed on relevant limb segments to continuously capture a joint's biomechanical signature during daily life. The core innovation is the transfer of SHM's data analysis framework: algorithms that establish a baseline 'healthy' movement pattern for an individual's joint and then continuously compare new data to this baseline to detect subtle, early deviations indicative of degradation or inflammation, providing a quantitative, objective, and continuous metric for joint health far more sensitive than periodic clinical assessments or patient recall.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune Modulation: Testing a 'Firebreak' Strategy for IL-17A Inhibition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that complete IL-17A inhibition is always beneficial by drawing an analogy from forest fire management, where total fire suppression paradoxically increases the risk of catastrophic blazes. The analogous solution is the use of controlled, prescribed burns to reduce fuel load and create firebreaks. Adapted to immunology, the research idea is to design a preclinical or clinical study comparing the long-term infection risks of complete, continuous IL-17A blockade versus an adaptive modulation strategy. This strategy could involve intermittent dosing, tissue-targeted inhibition, or concomitant prophylactic regimens aimed at preserving critical host defense functions at barrier sites (the 'firebreak'), testing if this approach mitigates severe or opportunistic infection risk while maintaining control of IL-17A-driven pathology.

ideas.title: Precision restoration of mucosal IL-17 immunity in stratified CMC patients
ideas.description: This research program first establishes a patient stratification platform using deep immunophenotyping and functional assays on patient-derived cells to categorize CMC by specific defect (e.g., Th17 cell deficiency, impaired cytokine signaling, or excessive regulation). It then develops and tests targeted therapeutic vectors, such as engineered IL-17A-producing TRM cell transplants or localized cytokine-releasing biomaterials, designed to correct the identified defect specifically at the mucocutaneous barrier. The approach includes integrated safety modules using concurrent anti-inflammatory biologics or suicide genes to control potential off-target inflammation, ensuring a robust therapeutic window not afforded by systemic IL-17A enhancement.

ideas.title: Adaptive Convolutional Networks for Radiographic Damage Scoring Using Satellite Change Detection Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from remote sensing—where machine learning models excel at detecting subtle, progressive changes in satellite image time series (e.g., deforestation, coastal erosion)—to the problem of scoring joint damage progression from radiographic images. The analogous solution uses specialized convolutional neural networks (like Siamese networks or change detection CNNs) trained to highlight meaningful structural changes between temporally separated, coregistered images while ignoring irrelevant variations. Adapted to radiology, this involves developing a deep learning model that takes a pair of coregistered baseline and follow-up joint radiographs as input. The model's architecture and training objective would be specifically designed to output a quantitative score of damage progression by directly learning the visual signatures of change indicative of disease advancement, such as new erosions or joint space narrowing, thereby enabling more sensitive, automated, and consistent longitudinal assessment in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis.

ideas.title: Analogous Engineering: Targeted IL-17A Enhancement via Feedback-Controlled Delivery
ideas.description: This idea investigates treating chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis by stimulating IL-17A, but adapts a solution from control systems engineering used in precision agriculture. In that field, sensor-based feedback systems enable precise, on-demand delivery of nutrients to correct deficiencies without waste or toxicity. The adapted research idea is to develop a closed-loop, feedback-controlled system for IL-17A enhancement. A biosensor would continuously monitor the site of infection for fungal biomarkers. When levels rise above a set point, the control unit would activate the localized delivery of an IL-17A-stimulating therapeutic, creating a targeted 'immune boost' precisely when and where it is needed to clear the infection, thereby scaling up the protective immune response while potentially avoiding the pitfalls of chronic, systemic IL-17A elevation.

ideas.title: Ecological Network Stability Analysis for Microbiome Dysbiosis Under IL-17A Inhibition
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from theoretical ecology to the original gastroenterology problem. In ecology, the risk of species invasion after a perturbation (like removing a key species) is predicted by analyzing the structural stability of the community's interaction network. The analogous solution involves modeling species interactions to calculate metrics like 'invasibility niche' size. Adapted to microbiome research, the idea is to analyze gut microbiome changes under IL-17A inhibition by constructing and perturbing microbial interaction networks derived from metagenomic and metabolomic data. The adapted research will quantify the destabilization of this network—specifically the expansion of the niche favorable for invasion—to provide a mechanistic, model-based biomarker for candidiasis risk, offering a more predictive tool than simple taxonomic shifts.
